Name: 56_copilot_github copilot_code_github
Representative_Docs: ['as someone who has spent a considerable amount of time in the software industry over time i ve always been on the lookout for tools and techniques that can help me boost my productivity reduce bugs in my code write less but achieve more i wanted to share some ai tools that have helped in programming in this subreddit as it could be helpful for some people who are learning to code i ll share my personal experiences with each tool including their strengths and weaknesses copilot by github github copilot is an ai powered code assistant that helps you write code faster i ve used copilot with typescript javascript dart and python there were moments when it felt like it read my mind and generated exactly what i wanted it was amazing however these moments were rare like few times in a month most of the time its performance was hit or miss it doesn t know your codebase and often guesses function names incorrectly there were instances where it created code with subtle bugs which forced me to spend extra time analyzing its output despite these shortcomings copilot is pretty decent at generating simple repetitive patterns and auto completing documentation gpt engineer gpt engineer is an ai tool that promises to speed up the app development process i decided to test it by trying to create an express app using gpt at first it seemed promising it laid out a clear architecture chose the right frameworks and even structured the code neatly but the excitement was short lived as the code it churned out was below par and i couldn t get the app to start i thought upgrading to gpt might help and while it did give slightly improved results it still fell short of creating a fully functional app so while gpt engineer shows promise it s safe to say it s not quite up to handling serious coding tasks just yet cody ai cody ai is an ai powered coding assistant that i ve been using in vscode for some time now it s transformed my coding experience in several ways it s excellent at breaking down code blocks into simple summaries this is super handy when i m reviewing code from other projects or need a quick refresher on my own work it s clever at filling in the blanks in log statements error messages or code comments it cuts out the need for copy pasting by filling in gaps for common patterns surprisingly it s pretty good at creating tests however when writing in markdown its suggestions tend to be long winded and always positive getting a negative sentence out of it is almost impossible also its inline suggestions can sometimes be a nuisance as it doesn t really get your code despite these drawbacks cody ai has been a huge help when translating my code into english and constructing tests making it an indispensable tool in my toolkit react agent react agent is an ai tool designed to help with building react components i tried using it to build a basic state management component for a react app it did churn out some code that i could use but it frequently missed out on some of my specifications or dropped features that it had added earlier it required a lot of hand holding and attention to detail which didn t save me much time as it stands now the code produced by react agent isn t ready for production and needs a good amount of tweaking before it can be merged into an existing codebase but it overall increased my productivity v by vercel v is an ai tool that generates ui designs however in my experience it creates mediocre ui with questionable usage of tailwind css anything built with v either needs heavy modification or ends up looking like an amateur product i do applaud the effort but ui designs are intricate and dynamic we re not quite at the point where ai can consistently produce top notch ui designs yet but it gives you a starting point for your ui sweep ai this tool tackles the biggest issue i ve faced with development with ai assist giving context to the existing app source when making new requests the feature of delivering the output through a pr is a neat addition i ve already made a few prs using this sure i had to make minor adjustments manually before merging them but it certainly saved me a good half an hour scriptgpt scriptgpt is a tool i created which is designed to offload feature development to an ai agent powered by gpt it s tailored specifically for ts js projects automatically installing required libraries testing code adding comments and more unlike other ai powered coding tools like github copilot and gpt engineer that struggle with effective code integration and building complex projects scriptgpt excels in these areas it can be used alongside these tools for writing code while offloading specific project features to scriptgpt as the creator of this project i might be a bit biased in my assessment while it s not perfect and there s always room for improvement i truly believe that scriptgpt can be a valuable addition to a developer s toolkit give it a try and see how it can improve productivity github repo in a nutshell ai tools aren t perfect yet they sometimes make mistakes and they can t always understand what you are trying to do but they re getting better all the time in the future they will be more powerful and helpful they ll be able to understand your code even better and they ll be able to generate even more creative ideas share your thoughts i d also like to hear your thoughts suggestions i m always looking to improve', 'eh the verdict is still out the study tracked around developers comparing their output with and without github s copilot coding assistant over three month periods surprisingly when measuring key metrics like pull request cycle time and throughput uplevel found no meaningful improvements for those using copilot in fact the study found that developers using copilot introduced more bugs into their code according to cio uplevel also saw no evidence that the ai assistant was helping prevent developer burnout i ve been using it since day and i honestly use it less and less as time goes on the code it generates just isn t that great i turned copilot off as well as my ide auto complete is more accurate', 'the numbers are misleading i use copilot and of the code copilot write is basically autocomplete a lot is exactly what a non ai autocomoplete would do some is slightly better because it autopopulate parameters based on context and sometimes also get it right sometimes is worse because it hallucinate method call that don t exists the reason i keep it there is because it does a lot of boilerplate well it s not often that i use it to actually write code occasionally very occasionally i put the name of the function and based on context it does a good guess at what the function should be but most of time is just not what i wanted and just delete all the content of the function copolilot added statistic will count all the autocomplete and code i deleted as code written by copilot and only count the letter i actually typed as code written by me if i write the first three letter of a method and copilot autocomplete the whole method passing the parameters based on context i wrote statistic will say copilot wrote of that line but while technically true is not exactly what people here when they listen copilot wrote of the code when people here that think that of the code is something copilot came up with and was my input while in reality is my input and copilot just typed it for me']

Name: 58_project_css_learn_code
Representative_Docs: ['i m currently on my self taught journey so while i m far from a paragon of knowledge when it comes to programming i ll be the first to tell you i don t know shit i m at the point where i can look back at how i started learning and see what mistakes i made and the mistake is a very common one that s very well known but doesn t become apparent until you experience it don t focus on tutorials tutorials familiarize you with how technologies work but building projects familiarizes you with how to work technologies react didn t make a lick of sense of me when i first started learning it but when i built my first project i learned x more than i did when i was watching hours of udemy videos feeling like crying because i couldn t grasp what the hell a prop was so in light of that i m gonna help you mr or mrs beginner with a list of starter noob friendly projects that you can use to spur your imagination the point of these projects are to get you out of tutorial hell but also they re scalable as you learn more you ll be able to revisit these projects and add more and more functionality eventually you can even add them to your portfolio and use it to get jobs just make sure you actually care about whatever the project is this list will try to be language amp framework agnostic although my experience is certainly influenced by the fact that i only know react so keep that in mind also this is just a post meant to get your creative juices flowing i can t say these projects will get you a job i don t even have one myself yet and i m not trying to position myself as a know it all influences just hoping to help a newbie who s looking for it since i often felt alone when i was beginning things on my own lastly bear in mind i m learning web dev so my suggestions will be biased towards that anyway here s what i put together amp x b create a recipe app this is a tried and true cookie cutter tutorial project but there s a lot of ways you can mix it up you can use custom measurements like your favorite spoon you use to measure you can add functionality that allows you to filter the recipes by what you have in your fridge you can add all sorts of ways to sort your data like sorting by healthy or by favorites or by recipes without dairy and so on if you re into fitness combine it with a calorie tracker or protein tracker create something you can see yourself actually utilizing in your day to day life create an app to manage your mental health pull in yoga poses from an api make a component that shows you different coping strategies Representative_Docs: ['require and check citations chatgpt often generates citations of articles that were never written in fake journals', 'ask them to insert in text citations chat gpt does not do that if they are unable to cite every sentence in their assay you can prove they used ai also chatgpt makes up citations even when you specifically ask it to provide the source of a specific sentence it will give you a seemingly legit reference but when you look for it you are unable to find it', 'sure i picked a couple of articles i d published and asked it to make citations pretty much as you described twice it tried crediting the editor of the volume as the author then started hallucinating elements of the publication as i tried to guide it towards the actual citation as another test i gave it the url for a translation i have on my personal website as a pdf no matter what i did chatgpt wouldn t use the title of the anonymous work as the first element of the entry but insisted that i am the author with each attempt to correct course it invented new details eventually it made an entry that was almost perfect except that it included a subtitle for the work not found anywhere in the pdf a student or professor who knows enough to tell when chatgpt s output is correct would be able to make a citation on their own without that help i m not too worried about them everyone else will use chatgpt the way they ve used other bibliography generators for years making citations that don t accurately represent the source cited and they ll think they ve done the work when they haven t those are the ones i worry about since they don t understand that every reference in their document is wrong when the bibliography points to a different version than they one they quoted for example or when their bibliography doesn t contain enough information to identify the source at all']

Representative_Docs: ['on a more lighthearted note relatedly i ve received a number of administrative emails from students that were clearly written with chatgpt s help that s not cheating of course then worked with him on his request i use chatgpt in just about every facet of my job from writing code to generating slide designs and images i ve been building a few gpts to help me out in some really repetitive tasks', 'here is chatgpt s attempt at creating such an assignment prompt write an assignment that instructs students about how emails are usually framed within professional settings and what they don t include e g information already given to them such as information in the syllabus assignment in professional settings emails serve as a primary means of communication it is important to frame emails appropriately in order to convey a clear and concise message when writing emails in a professional setting it is important to use a clear and professional subject line the subject line should clearly summarize the purpose of the email and should not be overly long use a professional greeting begin the email with a professional greeting such as dear name or hello name use a clear and concise message the main body of the email should be clear and concise it should include only necessary information and should not include any unnecessary or irrelevant details use a professional closing end the email with a professional closing such as sincerely best regards or thank you it is also important to keep in mind what should not be included in professional emails personal information professional emails should not include any personal information such as your age religion or political beliefs casual language or slang professional emails should use formal language and avoid slang or colloquial expressions emoticons or emoji while these may be acceptable in personal communication they are not appropriate in professional emails information already provided if the necessary information has already been provided such as in a syllabus or previous communication it is not necessary to include it again in the email by following these guidelines you will be able to effectively communicate in a professional manner through email']

Name: 75_web_development_web development_dev
Representative_Docs: ['gt what else is there besides web development software engineering in a more general sense that means developing systems that aren t related to web applications aerospace embedded systems operating systems machine learning defense logistics enterprise software consumer software cybersecurity cryptography industrial systems manufacturing and mechanical systems writing software for a power plant or cars and planes and drones or software ecosystems that power day to day work and infrastructure in large companies the list goes on forever gt what is web development web development is software development pertaining to websites and web applications that could mean anything from the web pages you re looking at now to the underlying systems that power them such as how data flows through them the processing and number crunching to produce that data etc also worth mentioning app development is a sister to web development these days because of the overlapping ecosystems and user space gt why is it so popular two big reasons because it s in your face everywhere it s the first thing people think of because it s what most average people and developers alike spend most of their time interacting with in a day because it s much easier to break into the space and start as a beginner or even find a good job without an engineering degree you don t need the same proficiencies in mathematics physics computational theory and understanding of low level hardware for web development that you do for things like aerospace embedded systems cybersecurity etc web development is also much easier to freelance in and learn quickly because of the existing ecosystem of open source frameworks and tools also web development typically takes more manpower than other niches so there are just a lot of web developers in comparison to other niches', 'sure there are a lot of potential development specialties so i ll cover some of the most common most development nowadays is consumer or business applications deployed to web or mobile in that environment you have the following specialties frontend specializing in development of uis in the browser the core skills here are javascript typescript css and javascript frameworks react angular vue the key here is getting to know the web environment really well truly understanding web layout styling the js engine and application state management you also need a basic understanding of backend technologies to be able to interface with the server but can get away with not being an expert most frontend devs also have some amount of design ux skill as they are typically the ones working most closely with designers to ensure a good user experience backend specializing in development of the server side data processing that powers applications a backend dev would typically know several backend languages java go c python ruby etc with their corresponding web service frameworks spring asp net rails etc you also need strong database expertise knowledge of deployment tools docker kubernetes etc and at higher seniority distributed systems design fullstack a very controversial term in general it means someone who s competent both frontend and backend but what competent means varies wildly from company to company at some companies this is frontend who can also write a crud endpoint at others it s backend but could add a simple button at others it s real cross hitting there s also a general frustration among engineers with companies expecting people to know everything which often leads to people who really don t know what they are doing cobbling things together devops responsible for building and maintaining the tools that allow you to build and deploy your code and keep it running in productions key skills are ci cd tools like jenkins deep knowledge of cloud platforms like aws and azure deep knowledge of container technology like docker and kubernetes networking and usually a bit of infosec knowledge also needs to know underlying tools like operating systems and databases well usually fluent in scripting languages like python has a lot of overlap with backend often a little better paid because of supply demand issues note that devops generally also has much more time on call than other specialties so be ready to be paged at am mobile exactly what it sounds like builds mobile apps can either specialize in android or ios or do both like frontend often needs a fair amount of ux design skills key skills are all about mobile platforms swift and or kotlin java and the huge toolkits that support mobile development generally higher paid again for supply demand reasons qa test engineering a role that varies a ton by company at some companies it doesn t exist at others it s a manual testing role that s a second class citizen and at others it s a full specialty focused on the development of testing tools and processes tools engineering you build tools that help other developers do their job better generally only exists at large companies engineers can overlap heavily with devops database administration generally exists either at older companies or bigger ones is all databases all the time with a focus on performance and security security super varied by company at some places this is essentially a devops specialty focused on security including penetration testing security auditing security practices and processes and of course incident response at other places it s a non dev role if you go into this field make sure you know exactly what kind of work you want to do and find a company thay has those sorts of roles business intelligence data analysis focusing on reporting key skills are sql data warehouse technologies and various bi tools tableau amplitude etc not really a development role per se but adjacent data engineering concerned with getting data especially analytics data into the right place often responsible for building data warehouses key technologies include databases kafka and message queues data warehouse tech like redshift and big data tools like the hadoop ecosystem data science depending on the company this is either something done by people with real degrees in statistics and or mathematics or it s a fancy word for a bi analyst it is a major problem that companies hire experts in statistical modeling and or machine learning and then basically have them run reports as a self taught person this field isn t really open to you additionally there are developers who build things other than standard web applications including the folks who build operating systems desktop software embedded systems and a million other things these specialties are a lot less accessible to self taught folks since they generally require knowledge of lower level languages and systems', 'both machine learning and web development are such broad fields that it s hard to generalize in a way that really does them justice i m going to do my best to provide a general perspective based on what i ve heard from friends in the field and my own experience web development everyone s favorite punching bag for some reason people in cs have this superiority complex about web development and it gets this negative reputation because it s easy and even bootcamp grads can do it or something like that while there is a small grain of truth to this for the most part it s a pot of bullshit web development much like literally any other sub field of cs can be as easy or as difficult as the problems you re solving sometimes those problems can be easy in that you just need a basic page to display some information with html sometimes these problems can be difficult like serving millions of requests per second from a large set of distributed databases located all around the world technically both of the above examples are web development and the only stereotype about web dev as a career is that it s easier to break into than other sub fields because you can learn the basics via a short bootcamp instead of a year degree but make no mistake your average bootcamp grad probably wouldn t be able to work on the second example problem above generally speaking the day to day of a web developer involves building websites and or the backend technologies that power them some of the easier to break into work in this subfield that requires less of a algorithms or math background is the design and front end portion of the stack where one is designing the look and feel or user interface and experience of the site using front end languages like html and javascript typescript and popular frameworks like react and angular etc some examples of more technical work that are still web development are generally more on the back end this can involve a wide range of languages anything from javascript to python or c and generally speaking the day to day work involves creating the server side system that the front end interacts with to store the users state as well as interactions with a oftentimes separate database service to read and write data there s a bit of overlap here with cloud engineers because these servers generally run on cloud services and while i wouldn t lump them in together cloud related skills using aws azure gcp etc can be useful for a back end developer to have you ll also often hear the phrase full stack web developer thrown around and all it means is that it s someone who is comfortable with the languages and tools used for both the front and back end development a good way to get a feel for this kind of development is to build your own full stack web project from scratch which is something you can easily find tutorials for on the internet machine learning riding the hype train arguably one of the most hyped sub fields of computer science machine learning is also an incredibly broad sub field in which the day to day work can vary greatly in addition it has a reputation of being fairly difficult to break into unlike web development because of the high math technical background required more on this in a minute i also personally think that the day to day work of an mle is one of the most misunderstood things and that most students tend to think it s far more glamorous than it actually is but that s neither here nor there the best way to describe the day to day work of an ml engineer is to break out what types of work require certain levels of education a lot of people who are into machine learning really want to be doing the cool stuff which usually means they want to be implementing deep learning models using a framework like pytorch or tensorflow the reality is that a fresh grad role doing any kind of ml engineering is unlikely to have very much model implementation if any in addition most roles and companies don t really require sexy deep neural networks for their data analysis and generally speaking in normal industry jobs you ll be working with linear models fancy linear models and decision trees in addition most of the work of an ml engineer actually has less to do with ml and more to do with data think of it this way if you re working on a team at a large company you ll only really need to experiment write the model once but you will need to re train the model with more new data all the time so most of the work that actually goes into running production grade ml at scale is around building data pipelines to train your existing models faster and better and with inference pipelines to serve the machine learning models to your end users the actual underlying model architecture and code is likely written once and the person doing that part of the job will probably have a phd so realistically working as an ml engineer with only an undergraduate degree ends up being more like working as a data engineer building data pipelines for training and inference of ml models at scale the core languages are python and hot take here sql there s this awesome blog post by vicki boykis that i think does a far better summarizing than i can here so i ll just link it but it s really fantastic and you should give it a read if data science ml is something you re interested in a note on salaries you specifically asked about salaries so i ll just comment briefly at the high end big n everyone makes a lot of money and it s mostly the same the ml folks might make a little bit more just because the demand is kind of high right now but i think some really specialized web dev folks are probably competitive generally speaking at big n the salaries are determined by your level and not your specific sub field role however once you go outside of big n i think there s a lot more room for variability ml is sexy right now so it will probably pay more on average but again that s a hugely sweeping generalization pay also fluctuates with the location quite a bit your best bet for getting good salary info once you have an offer are and the cscq salary sharing threads my own experience i don t have a graduate degree yet i work in ml at a self driving car company obviously this is in stark contrast to what i said above but i m discreet would be appreciated tia', 'i copied and pasted your query into chatgpt and this is what it came up with this kind of tactic embedding gibberish in white text is a known form of academic dishonesty and plagiarism evasion and in some cases it s a primitive attempt to fool ai detectors or turnitin style plagiarism checkers here s a breakdown of the likely motivations and implications what s going on the student added white font gibberish text that s invisible on a white background unless highlighted or copy pasted into the document this has been observed in several contexts ai detector manipulation some students mistakenly believe that inserting random human like text even gibberish can reduce the chance of being flagged by ai detectors like gptzero or turnitin s ai detection they think it will confuse the detector by breaking up large chunks of ai generated prose plagiarism avoidance in past years students sometimes used hidden text like long quotes or nonsense to try and dilute the percentage of matched content in plagiarism checkers ai tool artifacts in rare cases students copy paste from low quality ai generators or websites that embed hidden text or tokens for watermarking tracking or even seo search engine optimization reasons that s more common with shady free ai websites attempted justification the student s excuse he did it to prove he wrote it himself doesn t hold water adding white font gibberish doesn t prove authorship it obscures it it s more often associated with trying to hide something not reveal it how to interpret it you re very attempt to obscure authorship or deceive detection software the gibberish white text may look like repeated characters or keyboard mash asdkfjasdf lklklklk qwernmxcv pasted song lyrics famous quotes or paragraphs from other sources garbled or multilingual fragments even emojis or code snippets what you can do here are a few ways to respond or follow up highlight and save the white text paste it into a plaintext editor to preserve what was hidden use it as potential evidence in your academic honesty process ask the student to explain ask them directly can you explain how this proves you wrote it yourself their explanation may reveal misunderstanding or intentional deception review submission history if you re using canvas blackboard or turnitin check the submission metadata or revision history if available some lmss track if a file was uploaded from google docs etc which might offer more clues reassess past work if this student has submitted suspicious work before this may be part of a broader pattern you might request a brief oral explanation of their essay ai users often falter here final take the student s action strongly suggests an intent to deceive rather than prove originality white font gibberish is not a common or legitimate form of authorship verification it s much more aligned with cheating behavior even if poorly executed if you d like i can help you users inaccurate information sometimes rooted in prejudice while many of these issues have since been addressed and resolved it s easy to see how even in the best of circumstances the use of ai tools can have unforeseen and undesirable consequences']

Name: 88_ml_machine_machine learning_learning
Representative_Docs: ['decision trees linear and logistic regression are all very common tools probably even more so than deep learning but deep learning is used very intentionally for a lot of the heavy lifting in ml applications deep learning is most appropriate when you have practically infinite compute resources e g server side and performance is the most important concern but it may not be best if you care about explainability or have strict resource limits e g speed latency battery life disk space etc that s a shifting landscape as theory catches up to practice in neural networks and better technology develops to support deep learning on mobile but it will basically always be true that a small linear model is faster and more compact than a state of the art deep learning model in production ml the goal is to make a useful feature or system and to do so you make trades between many different constraints in research ml the goal is to get published so it s most important to outperform a benchmark in order to show that your technique represents an improvement if i can get of the performance of a state of the art deep learning solution from a well tuned linear model then i m definitely going to at least start by building the feature around a linear model we can always go back and replace it with deep learning but the linear model is easier and faster to build and test and the marginal improvement is probably unnecessary', 'uh lol it can t tell you why it came up with the answer that sentence illustrates your complete naive perception of machine learning first machine learning isn t about getting the answers the paradigm is basically this normal programs take data and rules to provide an answer like a calculator machine learning takes data and answers and tries to provide rules think decision trees next outside of neural nets it s not terribly difficult to determine what s happening in an algorithm regression just a linear function the entire body of regression analysis is about knowing the how and why of regression and executing a set of steps to come up with a statistically supported stable repeatable relationship between features clustering most are just moving centroids around in some fashion and measuring some degree of fitness of the clusters as the data scientist you should know your data subject field and the algorithms well enough to identify meaningful performance measures ahead of time the rest is just selecting an optimization method for drawing boundaries around the data classification very basic splitting strategy and a performance metric probability just math time series just extrapolating additive or multiplicative components of a process series and assessing moments to approximate a range of likely forecast values literally just math again all the other nuanced variations on these common methods are just optimizations for the sake of it or they are very specific to a field this sentiment that machine learning just spits out answers when you give it data is the product of bootcamps and moocs oversimplifying the process and convincing mbas they can be data scientists in three months because they can pump a historic bitcoin price csv through automl this is where so much bad data science comes from and why it is actually valuable to have an advanced degree in the field and formal education in theory the harder are neural nets because the math is extra it s not so much that we don t fundamentally understand them it s that a large network has so many connections explaining what is happening step by step would defeat the purpose of the whole thing because it would take longer than just doing whatever it s doing by hand what should be remembered is that your aren t using the nn for the answers you re training it to build a set of weights that are essentially a set of rules given the architecture in that you have a classifier trial and error is really just experimentation that s proper data science it s not blind trial and error though there is a method and following empirical methodologies leads to results that are repeatable stable and mathematically supported the hype is going to die down because a bunch of hacks have been getting into the field and applying whatever the algorithm flavor of the week is on their mooc and got bad results they didn t know where invalid because they don t actually know what they re doing', 'deep learning falls under ml which falls under ai i m not an expert but here s how i understand it ai is all about intelligent algorithms breakthroughs in early ai had to do with basic chatbots and game players and under the hood a lot of early ai had to do with graph searching algorithms the techniques were mostly in the wheelhouse of a discrete math loving computer scientist machine learning is all about algorithms that learn from data typically there s an input data set that gets analyzed by an algorithm known as training and then an algorithm can look at new data and categorize it in a particular way popular machine learning include things that are relatively simple like decision trees and markov chains as well as things that are more popular now like support vector machines and neural networks the techniques here are more of a blend between discrete math linear algebra calculus and statistics now in machine learning you come in with an input data set and end up learning how to classify the data or something however you can have the same data essentially represented in different ways e g maybe data is in polar coordinates vs rectangular and you might come up with different results in your machine learning model depending on your data representation there might be a representation of data that can best be used by models to classify information deep learning is about stacking different machine learning models on top of each other so that one part of the model can learn the best representation of your input data and the next part learns to classify that information in particular neural networks are interesting for this approach because they inherently are thought of in a layered way deep learning is very popular today as an area of research and it s thought to be particularly useful in areas related to image and video analysis']

Name: 89_resume_linkedin_recruiter_recruiters
Representative_Docs: ['alright here goes so first and foremost i am not a recruiter but i have done hiring and looked over a lot of resumes so this is just one guys opinion in my opinion there are two stages in the thought process of a recruiter when they pick up a resume is it worth my time to look at this resume for more than seconds i have decided based off my second analysis that this resume is worth actually reading to determine if this person is qualified in my opinion your resume fails number one and if you don t pass number one you can t pass number two here s why recruiters love buzz words on your resume you have aws kuburnetes react terraform devops sql etc all super hot technologies methodologies right now impressive for a junior dev recruiters don t know what those things are but they know that they want them the problem is every single one of those buzz words are on the second page of your resume of the time they are never going to get that far i think this resume would be far stronger as a page resume how could you do that i think profile sections on resumes are distracting and overrated in most cases i think the profile section can either be condensed to sentences max or completely replaced by just putting a title next to your name on the resume student full stack engineer or something like that if you really want it put it in a cover letter or something recruiters get s of resumes a day and there s no way they are reading those walls of text i know i m not i didn t read yours i would also consider removing the casual academic position to help condense this resume whatever you have to do to get that technical skills section to the main page another great thing to have in your experience section is any quantitative metrics you can come up with how many users did you scale to what was the uptime of your project etc a side note on linkedin a few weeks ago i contacted an old friend who works at apple to get a referral she works in recruiting she went to my linkedin to check it out and then chose to give me some unwarranted advice i m glad she did my linkedin experience section basically just had a bunch of descriptions of what the company did abc is an ai company specializing in b b blah blah blah anyway she told me to plug in what i did accenting all the technologies i used basically make those buzz words super visible so i went in and wrote one brief sentence about what the product was and like a full paragraph about how the front end was built in react and what aws technologies i used basically dropping buzz words left and right and i noticed a drastic increase in recruiter emails like drastic drastic i think this advice works for experience sections on resumes as well at any rate a lot of this is just my opinion but i hope it helps good luck', 'i started searching in my junior year and i also landed my full time job before graduating in may i know how tough the full time job intern market is many of my friends are still struggling to find jobs we have similar bg but i m the only one who received the offers i know i m lucky but i also know it s because i prepared smarter and worked harder i was still revising my resume during the winter holidays i focused on maximizing the efficiency of three key steps in the job search process resume job search apply and interview prep intern searching applications indeed linked in job postings are too competitive a job posted just day ago may already have applicants even after uploading your resume many platforms still require manual input i m talking about you workday how does this company still exist diversified job application websites i prefer handshake over indeed because it corporated with universities and many companies are directly linked to their official websites easy apply on linkedin or indeed might not be seen by recruiters a more effective way to apply is by submitting your resume directly through the company s website you can google the company s name and go to their careers page to find the position i also consider the latest job postings from startups which are generally more welcoming to new graduates and often don t post jobs on linkedin or indeed i followed many startup founders on linkedin most of them like to post job openings directly on their pages interview preparation as a student who has student loan i couldn t afford any career coaches mocking with college peers wasn t helpful they couldn t provide valuable feedback i need useful advice from industry professionals also finding real interview questions was frustrating i was tired of manually collecting them from google i checked past candidates reviews on glassdoor and combined them with ama interview s real interview question banks this helped me better understand the interview process and potential questions for my target roles allowing me to prepare a solid interview cheat sheet after going through multiple interviews i realized that many of the questions are quite similar not only phone screens behavior questions but also technical stages and case study i fully utilize their real time feedback to identify issues only face to face mock can detect such as lack of eye contact which can make me look less confident something should be avoided in interviews resume cover letter chatgpt is useful when i provide my resume and jds and ask it to tailor my resume to the position but i don t know how well it s actually revised still where can i get feedback where can i get a cover letter that doesn t sound overly ai but more human i listed my relevant internship experience company name title and what i had done shown in star format and gave chatgpt the resume draft along with the job role asking it to tailor the resume for the position then i gave the revised version to chatgpt asked for feedback on my resume s suitability for the role and revised it again and again until it got the highest score change the general role to a specific position if you want to tailor it for a specific company s role but still remember tools only improve your efficiency they don t replace real knowledge and hands on experience for students still in school try to work on as many projects as possible in your area of interest and gain as many internships as you can real work experience will set you apart from other candidates hope everyone can make it through the internship full time job market successfully', 'you ve applied to dozens of jobs double checked your qualifications you ve heard nothing other than what seems like an auto rejection you aren t alone job seekers say the rejection comes too fast it has to be automated i m qualified so what am i doing wrong employers insist i ve used every major ats none of them auto reject resumes not even at the biggest companies so what gives let s unpack what s actually happening why your resume might never be seen how to fix it and what you can do to get more interviews with less frustration this is compiled from years of personal experience in hr tech with quotes from recruiters and hiring managers you got knocked out before anyone saw your resume most companies use knockout questions during the application process one wrong answer and you don t meet their qualifications common questions do you need sponsorship yes rejection what s your location too far rejection expected salary too high rejection some ats pre fill various questions for you after parsing your resume as a time saver make sure all of these details are correct if you are applying to a job in dallas but your location says fort worth you may unnecessarily be sent the rejection pile why some companies only review applicants within miles of the office for in person or hybrid roles your resume is missing keywords key experience and skills you might be qualified but your resume has to prove it recruiters use keyword searches to find top applicants if your resume doesn t include the exact words they are looking for you might not show up at all we have a few seconds to scan each resume if we don t see the right keywords we move on they are looking to see if you have done the job before and how great you did the job don t make them guess here s where it can be tricky sometimes even overqualified candidates get filtered out because their resume didn t spell things out hr doesn t necessarily understand all the intricacies of every role they are responsible for that s not their job for instance they might take requirements from the person or team leading tech or finance and create job postings out of that this creates a black and white scenario without much room for gray area examples you ve used microsoft azure ad but they ask for entraid it s the same thing rebranded but unless you list entraid hr might think you aren t qualified my wife a dnp fnp doctor of nursing practice family medicine once got rejected from a family nurse practitioner role for being underqualified why the recruiter didn t recognize the acronym for her degree once she spelled out that she was a family nurse practitioner she landed the next role each job has unique qualifications you need to tailor your resume and application for each one if you have the qualifications spell it out your content isn t selling your potential this isn t about keyword stuffing yes your resume needs to include the right keywords but they need to be in the proper context demonstrate you understand what they are looking for which are the key hard skills soft skills experience education and keywords they use throughout then show how you used that skill or showed that experience in an impressive and natural way strong bullets should answer what did you do how did you do it tools methods why did it matter results impact add names of programs software and systems was anything measured in values time saved performance gained or number of people impacted if you are a student you can highlight coursework volunteer experience or projects where you demonstrated what the employer wants you applied too late okay so you answered all knockout questions perfectly used all the right keywords your value is exceptionally demonstrated and you still didn t get an interview why if you applied to a job opening you expect to be considered right unfortunately that s not how ats work if you applied after too many people are already being considered you and your qualifications might not be noticed the ats sorts people based on the order they applied and if you are applicant but the hiring team found enough qualified people by applicant your application might not even be reviewed most hiring teams still manually review all of the applications this could be hundreds or thousands because this is such a manual time consuming process they start with the first applicants and only go as far as they need to in order to put a qualified shortlist together you might be the most qualified person in the entire pool and you won t even be seen if you applied too late wild right as other recruiters put it the default setting is to store applicants in order from the time someone applies so that recruiters can search and review most don t have smart filtering it s coming but it s not here tips apply as early as possible by setting alerts on linkedin google and company career sites on linkedin search jobs by past hours and tweak the url by changing tpr r to tpr r to show only jobs posted in the last hour your resume content will get you to an interview but being one of the early applicants will give you the chance of being considered it was a ghost job i hate this one but not every job posting means there s a real opening two common reasons they already have an internal candidate but have to post the job publicly even recruiters hate this my company does this all the time everyone goes through the motions knowing full well that john will be getting the job it wastes everyone s time and we all hate it but we have to do it they re not hiring yet especially after layoffs companies stockpile applicants for future openings they ll sit on qualified people to stay in the pipeline in case they get the green light to hire when they do they need to be able to bring people in quickly before their competitors do and due to the reasons above it s too time constraining and labor intensive to start from the beginning so they might make candidates wait in the process your resume format is hurting you when a recruiter scans your presentation needs to follow a simple pattern they are used to so they can easily locate key details to understand your qualifications anything that prevents this process from happening efficiently may leave you in the no pile some ats parse scan and store your resume details in part to auto fill some of the fields for you if you have poor formatting that prevents this process from happening correctly your details might be lost or in the very least populated incorrectly two or three column resumes are notorious for being difficult to scan correctly which can prevent key details from being found in relevant searches within the ats tips stick to single column layouts use plain fonts and standard headings no tables charts icons or design flourishes unless you re in a creative field a good resume should read like cliff s notes clear basic easy to read and retain the flashier your resume the worse your chances you re using ai the wrong way asking chatgpt to write my resume is a mistake why you ll sound like other applicants doing the same thing you risk fabricated details you lose your voice use ai to give you ideas as a jumping off point for writing research a company or role refine and shorten your language ai should polish your story not replace it if your writing shows the tell tale signs of ai recruiters might not trust that who you are on paper is who will show up to the interview if there is any doubt they may move forward with other candidates the ats isn t as smart as you think despite rumors most ats aren t powered by ai nor is ai auto rejecting candidates in reality ats are closer to filing cabinets with basic search capabilities many ats score resumes the scoring is terrible and most of the recruiters i know ignore them but it is a thing i ve used most of the big ones workday salesforce icims greenhouse jazz paycom beamery adp and they are generally speaking all pretty bad so we manually review applicants here are some basic facts features like knockout questions and keyword filters are set by humans recruiters search these systems browse candidates in the order they applied and humans make hiring decisions we are bound by compliance and are audited that no hiring decisions are being made by anything but a person i look at every single resume that applies to every one of my jobs and disposition every candidate swiftly if we re moving forward or not i work directly for a fortune company that has business units bottom line if ats were smarter all applicants would be seen and considered instead we are left with job seekers having to compete in a crowded broken system let s put it all together to dramatically increase your chances of getting hired tailor your resume to include all relevant keywords and experience demonstrate how you have the hard and soft skills they request use a simple resume template with outstanding content that is easy to skim apply early knowledge is power i m hoping this helps you navigate your job search more successfully any questions clarification or pushback i ve been corrected before and will happily edit this with good input']

Name: 95_llms_agi_llm_hype
Representative_Docs: ['there has been a big leap forward with llms but that isn t reason to think ai will continue advancing as fast from what i have seen llms aren t that applicable to programming even if they become more sophisticated llms are fun but we are absolutely in the hype phase their utility in general is being wildly over estimated', 'they didn t have the first component of many required for agi llms in the s according to yan lecun llm s alone are not sufficient for agi yan is throwing huge amounts of cold water on openai and every single ai company out there right now claiming their llm s are going to hit agi in years', 'i ll be polite on my disagreement because you were reasonable about what you said i literally work on llms for a living and am paid k year to do so when you say carefully navigating the infinite solutions space this isn t really a great representation at end of day llms are just probabilistic best next token generators the thing is while this sounds like they have no inherent understanding somewhat true that doesn t mean they can t excel at certain tasks take a chess engine for an example typically not an llm but still good example they may have no understanding at all of how chess works but even a simple model running on a phone can completely stomp the best human chess players creativity is also effectively just increasing noise from the very beginning llms have temperature field that basically controls that amount of variance and therefore amount of creativity if you slide this too far you get gibberish if you slide it all the way to left you get just the best expected token with little variance the other bit you re overlooking is that more powerful models is only a small area of investment this is what most people think of with llms foundation models like openai gpt o mini reasoning models like o mini o image models from other vendors etc these are continuously improving and i d agree i don t think that s sufficient to reach agi however this competely overlooks compound ai systems where you combine multiple models each specialized at certain tasks you can fine tune individual models to have lightweight models that are really good at one particular thing you can create rag systems that retrieve live data to feed into context so the model can have an up to date understanding of the world without retraining moore s law this is actually completely misleading here and for that matter moore s law stopped a while ago this is only dealing with transistors in software engineering terms vertical scaling we are well past this and ai systems rely heavily on horizontal scaling model training isn t done on one super beefy cutting edge cpu it s done on hundreds or thousands of h s gpu is already built on parallel processing i began my career in data engineering working with spark if moore s law was the limit we would not be able to run pipelines that process petabytes of data daily i won t comment as to whether agi will be a thing in next years or next years or next years i genuinely don t know i will say though with full respect that most people not working on this stuff genuinely have no idea what they re talking about and don t even understand where effort is being placed hopefully this didn t come off aggressive i appreciate your polite disagreement and wanted to share knowledge without coming off as antagonistic but i strongly disagree with your view here']

Name: 98_chatgpt_plagiarism_unethical_use chatgpt
Representative_Docs: ['is it cheating if you have strong understanding of the argument did the research yourself wrote down every point yourself collected the evidence from sources yourself but haven t edited each sentence with your own words to sound perfect i ve heard two takes on a chatgpt is cheating because you didn t write the paper b using it is not cheating since you actually did all the work and you re using chatgpt as your edit but not for ideas in other words you came up with everything yourself except the sentence and paragraph structure c neither specify your opinion in comment view poll', 'is it cheating if you have strong understanding of the argument did the research yourself wrote down every point yourself collected the evidence from sources yourself but haven t edited each sentence with your own words to sound perfect a chatgpt is cheating because you didn t write the paper b using it is not cheating since you actually did all the work and you re using chatgpt as your edit but not for ideas in other words you came up with everything yourself except the sentence and paragraph structure c neither specify your opinion in comment i feel like b myself because if you actually did the research yourself and know everything then putting your thoughts on thee paper is not stealing any ideas from anyone they re your ideas but you didn t waste time writing out each letter your mind however has the entire logic in it and you did the work searching and understanding everything', 'when i first saw chatgpt cited edit meant given authorship on a paper i thought huh that s weird but moved on not thinking about it but now that i think about it it makes no sense if someone believes chatgpt should be cited given authorship because it did the writing that to me basically says that a program which does much of the work of the research is contributing enough for authorship but to my knowledge it has never been commonplace to give authorship things like pcr machines that do a huge brunt of the work nor has it been common to give authorship to programs like r stata or sas which can be said to be doing all of the math and analysis if one believes chatgpt is doing the writing after all you give it the inputs prompts and review it s output just like you do when you write an r script to run regressions and i find myself wondering what message it sends about how we view the work of analysis vs the work of writing and how we view authorship most of us are taught from a young age that if you take someone else s words or otherwise don t write your own words that s plagiarism but there s no problems using a calculator unless told not to by a teacher we are trained to think that relying on machines to do quantitative or menial work for us but not to say that work is worth authorship is fine and expected but when using words that aren t ours we must credit similarly if you ve got a grad student toiling away in the lab or at a pc doing the actual quantitative or menial work it can be common especially in larger labs for that student to not get authorship but if the student actually wrote sections of the paper they are likely to get some form of authorship credit if we truly reach the point that we can have a machine do the writing portion of it for us as well we may find ourselves having to rethink what we define as an intellectual contribution worthy of authorship and many people who are doing actual labor to do the work of science hopefully may be able to get credit they are due but i won t hold my breath just as likely that things become more strict and only pis get authorship or something']

Representative_Docs: ['still not enough ai will teach the kids', 'i ve started coaching my kids and on ethical and smart use of ai that includes questions to ask the ai to test it s output and evaluate its sources it also includes that the foundational idea that your ai is only as smart and accurate as what you put into it if you re functionally illiterate and can t even write a paragraph to accurately describe what you need you re going to get garbage as a result if you contain a lot of technical knowledge logic skills and an ability to communicate clearly you can do awesome things with ai if you re uneducated well you are what you feed your ai i suppose there s no use in fighting it anymore the last generation tried to fight the internet and search engines that didn t work so they instead turned toward educating kids on smart use of search engines and evaluation of the content we just haven t hit that stage with ai in education yet there is no guidance no guardrails because frankly our leaders are so out of touch with what is going on and have no technical knowledge of what to do about it that leaves it to parents and teachers to teach kids the right way to use these tools and make it clear how lack of education is not going to get them ahead even in an ai world another way i like to frame it is you only have an advantage when you are one of the few that can use a tool what happens when everyone uses a tool that will be ai very soon your competitive advantage isn t going to mean much very soon and those who invest in learning and education on top of their technical skills are going to come out on top', 'wish my kids would at least use ai']

Name: 101_interview_interviews_interviewer_candidate
Representative_Docs: ['are companies forcing people to come into a locked down room with a computer sans web access to test their raw coding abilities before interviewing them or as part of the interview process on the other hand what do you say to people who made it through at least some of their required coding coursework only via getting help be it ai or other more traditional means and would not have passed without said help and are now applying for entry level positions out of university that think they ll just able to ai google stackoverflow their way through work too are we all in for a very rude awakening soon or have companies figured it all out and have ways of simultaneously keeping the ai addicts out while training people who have real potential but are just rusty on syntax', 'i ve interviewed a lot of candidates recently and in this age of rampant ai cheating you really should just trust your instincts if it feels off it probably is when people code for real they don t just let typos or obvious syntax errors sit there while they slam out more lines of code in fact people don t just slam out lines of code ever people don t glance back and forth while typing all of these things that feel very unnatural simply are unnatural i know this is just throwing gas on the fire that already was interviews favoring false negatives over false positives but i really think with the cheating tools available that s actually justified more than ever i try to reframe it like this even if i m wrong about someone cheating which to be clear i don t accuse people of this lightly if somebody is so awkward to work with that i suspect cheating i probably won t like working with them anyway maybe that s just a bad rationalization for allowing myself to be wrong about this sometimes but in the few times i ve actually come to the conclusion somebody was cheating in almost all cases i was right and was able to gather concrete evidence to support this before you ask how i asked one candidate to change his screen sharing mode from just this window to full screen and it messed up his cheat tool defenses and i literally saw it running another candidate accidentally shared the wrong screen and i saw he had a remote desktop utility running another candidate i suspected of cheating so i intentionally said some completely fake but realistic sounding techno babble and asked him to comment on it which he did with a totally straight face and plausible sounding albeit obviously fabricated answer', 'are companies forcing people to come into a locked down room with a computer passed without said help and are now applying for entry level positions out of university that think they ll just able to ai google stackoverflow their way through work too nothing pass my interview question then we ll talk are we all in for a very rude awakening soon or have companies figured it all out and have ways of simultaneously keeping the ai addicts out while training people who have real potential but are just rusty on syntax who is we candidate interviewer company those have very very different thought and objectives here']

Name: 103_manager_boss_team_company
Representative_Docs: ['i love coding i love solving challenging problems that solve real world problems i love making my own projects due to an unfortunate life circumstance where i could no longer afford rent with my old company s salary years ago i went job hunting and basically signed the first offer i got and it has been the worst experience ever one google search with the company name followed by the word fraud would have told me everything i need to know about this company besides the fact that they are in constant litigations they have fucked me over numerous times since i joined from the very get go they promised a position on this awesome high tech team and then bait and switched to what i would call a graveyard team where basically it s all old shitty tech and just constant refactoring old technology and clearly everyone on the team was there to do the bare minimum and collect a pay check no room for growth so i looked for a new team after a year finally found a new team and i was one of the first on the team we were building from the ground up i was ecstatic and became the lead without the title despite being the lowest level on the team everyone was coming to me for questions i was the guy to go to and i loved it i had a career manager cm who is responsible for my promotion except he is located on the team that i was supposed to join so he never actually saw the work i did i worked on my promotion case with him for months got great reviews from all my managers wrote up my performance report that took hours then one day he just left the company out of nowhere i think the company might have fucked him over somehow so he left he completely disappeared from the internet and sent me an email saying his cm will be my cm and he knows everything about my promotion so i scheduled a call with him and he knew nothing about it he s also a non tech guy so i was like oh great i was like wtf dude i ve been working on this for months so he said okay let me go talk to your team then he went and talked to the wrong person a middle manager lady who has no idea the work i do and she said i do not step up and take leadership or go above and beyond i told him that is not the right person to talk to and i have mentioned numerous times i wanted a leadership position and basically lead my team already anyways i have no idea why she even responded without reaching out to my manager first he said okay let me go talk to them again then weeks before promotions are coming up he emails me saying he s leaving on paternity leave for months i flipped shit i had no one to represent me in my promotion case anymore i did not get a performance review it just seems so disorganized i am literally the lowest level person on my team yet i have been the top performer it s completely fucked there s also other stupid shit like me getting in trouble for submitting a pr to our auth service that had a huge bug that caused the container to crash anytime you restarted it that every dev has to experience they called a person meeting to say not to submit pr for services that weren t what you were working on i was expecting a thank you but no i got reprimanded and that bug is still sitting there today yes they won t fix it even though i literally showed them how to fix it then just last week they gave the official lead title to someone who has never been leading the team like i have at this point i knew this company was never going to promote me tbh it seems like they have this system where they only promote this inner circle of each other and i m not in it at this point i don t give a shit i m collecting my paycheck and doing the bare minimum they show no appreciation to me whatsoever the ones who get recognition are the ones writing shitty code that barely functions but looks like they re doing a lot it s a complete circus and it s taking a toll on my mental health i played a huge role in building this product and have received recognition i ve obviously started interviewing for other companies but the interviews have been ridiculously hard i moved to sf from the other side of the country at possibly the worst time for the market and i am living paycheck to paycheck and it is causing lots of problems with my girl and i i had my meta onsite and after the first coding round it was clear to me they were setting me up to fail with the difficulty of the questions i guessed they hit their headcount for my level and i was right these entire past years have been complete shit for me both in work and in my personal life i lost my dog lost a family member to suicide and work wouldn t give me a single day off i feel hopeless i study so much and if i miss one question i get rejected i have my wall of shame where i post all my rejections to try and motivate me and it has grown quite a bit i have now been job searching for months and am basically back to square i don t know what to do anymore i feel depressed i used to love this industry but i hate the direction it is going corrupt greed is at an all time high they are trying to replace us with ai we are expected to know more every year while our salaries barely even move more and more everyday i am considering doing my own startup or joining a very early one but honestly i just don t fucking know anymore', 'i got a new job recently with a nice wage increase but in another city hr was very convincing about the culture of the company being flexible and fit for innovation and development i m a data scientist but now three months in i have the following situation my boss is really toxic he works overtime everyday keeps working at home and even during weekends he s called me lazy for not doing the same and has even got mad i told him not to bother me during weekends he s never wrong about anything and when i propose a different solution to a problem that s not his own he s some sort of solutions architect he gets mad and insists on his own and is extremely reluctant to my ideas he often mentions the month trial period as a sort of threat coupled with the fact that he think he can do everything by himself if needed things like you know i can decide what to do with you in months i have power over who stays in the team he gets really defensive when anyone threatens some of his power he s had very heated arguments with one other manager who was put to steer our team and with another guy in the team that shares some of the decision power one of this very heated arguments almost ended in a fistfight i think he s a fraud he claims he has instead of a full time employee he s the latter and what s worse is that my boss already put his boss against this guy so his fate rests entirely on whatever my boss decides i m not doing the job i was hired for i m a data scientist and was hired as such but right now i m more a sort of project manager for application design and my boss hired a duplicate role a machine learning specialist which he s giving all data analysis tasks that come up luckily i m pretty versatile so that i can work with software development otherwise i d be without real tasks the culture hr promised doesn t exist it m always forced to fill interface contracts or lengthy requirement specifications which seem designed to request features twice a year flexibility is minimum and i spend too much time in pointless meetings there is very high attrition hr even proposed to perform modeling on the data they have to determine the significant factors i was supposed to do it but my boss blocked it because he doesn t think it s worthy of our time or of the expertise of the team he blocks every request from other teams he doesn t personally like even if they re simple my boss moves strings to sabotage or reduce really interests me since i majored in cs and stats so i am more inclined to stay at company a if i moved teams i told my current manager about the startup and the offer from my old team he was very supportive and understanding since he also feels the legacy code re write is mundane and knows that i am very passionate about machine learning projects he spoke to his manager who was also supportive and they spoke to his manager who is in charge of the department i work in my manager s manager s manager s main concern is that i stay with company a instead of leaving so he was like i want you to be happy and do what you are passionate about so he would approve my transfer but asked me to decline the offer from the startup i agreed because he asked me so directly and i was just super nervous in retrospect i should have asked for a raise or promotion lol this all happened on monday and progressed much faster than i had expected i am very indecisive and was hoping to have more time to think about these options i have not told the startup about all this yet but have phone calls scheduled for later this week i don t know how to explain this to them since during the interview we had really good technical conversations and one of the phone calls this week is to discuss the details of a personal project on my github repo with the lead engineer in the mean time i also submitted applications to company c a big four and have first round interviews scheduled for this week as well i submitted it before my on site with company b but just heard back yesterday even though i told my manager s manager s manager that i would stay at company a if i could move teams but if i pass the interview with company c i would really like to work there i guess i can cross that bridge when i get to it i just feel like i m being super dishonest with company a and company b due to my inability to make and be happy with a decision since i m in the transition period at company a between my old team and my new team i am kind of doing two jobs finishing the stuff with my old team and starting the stuff with my new team with two stand ups each day i thought that moving teams would make me happier but now i m just more stressed at work and worried that even on the new team i still won t be able to do the real machine learning or big data stuff the grass is always greener i think that if i continue the interview with company c and get an offer from them that would make me happy working at a big four you know but in the back of my mind i feel that regardless the grass is just brown where i am']
