Name: 169_software engineering_se_software_engineering
Representative_Docs: ['computing science is more about theory and finding new ways to solve software problems software engineering similar but is more about applying existing knowledge and standard practices to solving problems cs prepares you for things like graphics programming physics engines ai coming up with new algorithms optimization and other research scientific type work where the solution isn t always known ahead of time software engineering on the other hand is typically using reliable and proven methods of creating and maintaining software systems software engineering is often the process of building software from idea to finished product and managing the development process cs is a bit less structured but allows you to explore in depth areas that you are interested in which are still changing and advancing through research and new technology software engineering is not so much about science and more about practical application and meeting specific criteria both degrees get roughly the same jobs after graduation but depending on your interests or the field you want to enter you may be more interested in one than the other for me personally i was interested in graphics and game development so i took computer science to learn real time rendering engines and fluid engines now i work for a software company using various technologies like opengl vulkan directx cuda and work on game engines which are frequently changing and improving each year cs is somewhat interested in the bleeding edge of what computers are capable of whereas software engineering applies only what is time tested and proven reliability and good practices are important here because you are delivering end products to businesses and consumers who need them to work consistently', 'hey i m a th year computer science student at university of manitoba your question is actually quite complicated let me explain broadly speaking computer science is the mathematics of computation software engineering borrows the useful bits of computer science and applies them to the real world to solve problems you can think of it as being the same relationship between something like physics and mechanical engineering now at most canadian universities a software engineering major is generally part of the engineering department while a computer science major is generally part of the science department at the u of m specifically this is where things get very confusing there is a software engineering specialization not major in the computer science degree but also in the computer engineering degree what are the differences well if you specialization in software engineering through the computer engineering degree you re going to learn a lot more about the hardware side of things and you re also going to be doing a ton of electrical engineering courses this degree is also fully accredited by the professional engineer council so you ll be able to legally call yourself an engineer and you get a ring the title p eng added to your name if you get the software engineering specialization through the cs degree you re going to get a more traditional education a focus on the software side of things like algorithms data structures etc and the ability to take some courses like artificial intelligence machine learning cryptography etc keep in mind software engineering is a specialization and not a major the majors you can pick are computer science or computer engineering personally specializing in software engineering through the cs degree is most likely going to be far easier and regardless of the path you choose you ll be working at the same jobs so i picked the cs degree for that very reason i don t care about calling myself a p eng or having a ring either summary software engineering at the u of m is a specialization in both cs and ce pick ce if you want to call yourself an actual engineer and focus on the hardware electrical engineering side of things pick cs if you want a traditional education with more of a focus on software cs will be easier and regardless of what you pick you will be working the same jobs also regardless of what you choose make sure you try and get into the co op program it s fantastic co ops are essentially paid internships and you can work for some pretty big companies google and amazon routinely recruit students through this and we also have ubisoft setting up a headquarters here and they are recruiting heavily i m in the co op program myself but have only worked locally interviews are extremely easy not very technical at all and there are generally far more job positions than students so if you get accepted into the co op program you re pretty much guaranteed an internship', 'first off i just want to establish that software engineering is a branch of cs just like ai security algorithms and others it s the branch of cs that deals with managing software projects and developing software products at some universities carleton for sure but there are probably others software engineering isn t even a program it s and option you can take with a cs degree anyway now that that s out of the way answer to your question depends what you want to do if you want to move up into product development and management roles software engineering is the way to go want to do algorithms ai computer vision cs is the way to go except neither of those statements are really true because there s so much overlap between the two programs always check the programs yourself this is a wide sweeping statement that may not be true everywhere that it really doesn t matter which you study both degrees will allow you to do any of those things listed above the main difference is that cs will give you more freedom you ll have more choice over what courses you take and se will be a more structured program with more mandatory course your software engineering courses personally i think those se courses are valuable even though it may mean not getting to take some classes that sound really interesting now i m probably a bit biased due to the fact that i m in se but i ve thought about switching to cs many times and i think i m coming at this objectively for me if you can take it software engineering is the better option for a few reasons software engineering is immensely important as i said in my other comment i m on co op right now and i m working on developing a piece of software for the company to use it s been mostly left to me to do from start to finish and going through the process has opened my eyes to how much more software development is than just learning how to use different technologies properly planning designing and managing a software project is key to delivering a finished product on time and on budget the bigger companies know this they ve been doing it a while that s why they re big again you can learn this stuff on the job and with experience but it makes you more valuable to just have it right out of the gate it s a more exclusive program a lot less people are admitted to se programs than cs giving your degree more value employers are going to see a lot of cs degrees se will set you apart from the crowd and tell employers you re coming to the company with the mindset of an engineer project and product focused on top of that not all universities offer se programs adding to the exclusivity basically ses are a rarer breed you can always take those courses you wanted to take but couldn t after you ve finished your degree no one forces you to leave after you ve completed your degree requirements nothing is stopping you from staying an extra semester to take one two three or more courses sure you could do that with the se classes but there are going to be reserved spots in those classes for the students in se and there might be no spots for students in other programs it might take longer than just one semester too it is waaaaaaay easier to switch from se to cs mid program than it is to switch from cs to se there are a limited amount of seats in all programs and engineering programs tend to have smaller limits my class is students science courses usually have much higher limits if any after admission engineering student culture can be cancer at some times but man it can be fun as hell too everyone hates the engineering faculty except for the nurses because we carry a fake sense of superiority but being on the inside is a blast there are usually tons of events workshops clubs and teams marketed to the engineering students there s a sense of camaraderie among the engineering students but i guess this last point is more of an anecdote and should be taken with a grain of salt anyway that s my case for se but cs is an equally good choice either way you ll probably end up applying for the same jobs and very likely doing the same things']

Name: 170_startup_company_big_team
Representative_Docs: ['job security is good to have nowadays in this economy i m lucky enough to work for a similar type company that has a tech stack years old there are no millennials at are company and no one wants to rock the boat at a fast paced moving startup you can get canned if manager is having a bad day but there is definitely more risk reward like ai self driving cars fintech or crypto startups you must be good since startups really want top people and poach from big companies big company you can blend in the background unless your manager is very hands on and aggressive tries to change the culture even interviewing at startups is nerve wracking since there is so much attention put on you and teams can be really small i wouldn t want to be under constant microscope all the time you get challenge at startup but right off the bat even if i was prepared and a good engineer the egos of startup engineers are very intimidating they reject people in terms of not being a good fit and it s very vague feedback i have been turned off by startup scene in general because of the tend towards hype twitter was cool to be working years ago years ago it was facebook years ago it was stripe and now every hot startup is an ai or fintech company i don t have numbers to back it up but i think like percent of vc money goes towards ai early seed companies chasing the golden goose there are good competent engineers at the mammoth companies and also somewhat lazy ones who just get in a rut and don t develop new skills the youngest person on our team is and in some startups you ll find that oldest person is a lot of it depends on the project and also if a big co wants to modernize it starts acting like a startup culture all hands on deck grind hustle hour days most people i meet want to clock out after pm and live their life', 'i been in the industry for years i have worked at enterprise firms startups medium sized firms only place i haven t worked at is big n working towards it right now i learned working hard for someone else don t really bring up your net worth that much at the ai tech startup i worked at i was working sweat and blood all day as full stack engineer two years ctos keep lying to me about nonexistent promotion i promoted myself by getting an offer from ibm with no intention of working there with k delta in salary and made the startup match the offer with threats of leaving during huge sprint after getting the promotion and senior title i jumped boat to my current company with another k pay bump the work i do here is extremely critical but easy compared to what i did at the ai startup lesson learned how much you get paid don t equate to your skills knowledge hard work rather it s correlated with revenue projects if you can grab the company at the revenue bottleneck point of sale ecommerce specific products you have both job safety and guaranteed promotion every year with threat of leaving obviously the above don t apply to companies bigger than medium sized enterprise and tech firms whom is smart enough to distribute the revenue bottleneck work also there is pay bottleneck at these non tech companies for texas i think it s around k for senior positions without transitioning into director these positions are relatively easy to get too because of low demand no one want to be wp admin and non leetcode interviews again if they do big n interview the position won t be filled ever however if you play your games smart you will make a lot more than the directors big n think about this way you spend min out of your hour workday getting salaried at k there s hr of potential for revenue from other areas these time can be spent by leetcoding preparing for your next big gig big n obviously pays way more as full time gig my favorite investing you can build solid fundamental portfolio which in years will beat the net worth of those who work at big n real estate you have plenty of time to look for good properties for rental side gigs contracts these are in the grey area try to not get spotted by your company as they can potentially take legal actions against you however if you keep it discrete you can get paid x as much spending time with your family if you wfh don t waste your life coding away these are just my findings at the end of the day you gotta ask yourself who do you work for if the answer is anything but yourself then you re a slave to the system there are tons of other non tech skillsets that pays way more than programming and you have to be smart to spot those', 'i m a nordstrom swe we just call ourselves engineer because screw your long titles like junior cloud application software development engineer in test and screw all the title obsessed people and holy crap is it amazing here i can t speak for credit karma so you will want to get that opinion elsewhere but i will vouch for nordstrom moreover i can only speak for hq ymmv because you re in a remote office at a big company but here s what i like about it great talent pool a lot of our hq engineers at least the more senior principal ones are ex amazon ex ms ex google ex ai etc so our hiring bar isn t crazy high but it s still relatively high and the thing about being in a tech hub is you ll attrach tech hub talent half my team is currently ex amazon and for a good reason but that s the thing about being at a seattle based company nordstrom feeds off of the tech giants and we have a pretty high ratio of ex tech giant people normal job normal people my only other frame of reference was a single big n company for years but in my experience that s where you get the hyper focused hyper competitive and type a people with very few hobbies outside of work here we have smart engineers but pretty good work life balance and it s really fun talking to my coworkers at lunch or even outside of work i ve met more people i d go to a concert here in months than at years at big n nordstrom nice if you re from the east coast this will annoy you but nordstrom nice is a thing and there are very few mean spirited people here and when you encounter one they kind of stick out like a sore thumb there are jerks at every company but there are fewer here imo and people here know how to be assertive without being a dick once again la might be different modern tech stack since this initiative to become a modern tech company is fairly new we are using all the latest tech aws gcp maven k s gitlab you name it we aren t betrothed to any legacy internal tools and just use standard means of messaging like https tls and public authorized apis we have modern dev ops patterns like micro services and proper alerting metrics and our own elk stack and all that i am not surprised that you guys are using scala since we are big on java but also big on using the best tool and aren t open to more hip languages like scala and go pretty good place to be as an e e i m an e so i mentor e s and because i m not overworked i never say to my mentee yeah sorry i m too busy there s a set path on getting promoted and once you get to e and you start designing systems there are some great resources at hq on getting design security feedback that help you build better systems next time anyways i ve been here months but the honeymoon period isn t over if i leave it will only be for more money they matched my big n comp but i d make more at big n simply because of stock appreciation but i m set for year minimum and i wouldn t be surprised if i stay here longer since i have no good reason to leave']

Name: 171_harvard_cs_edx_course
Representative_Docs: ['for any of these courses core computer science foundations programming cs s introduction to computer science harvard edx free verified cert available object oriented programming in java coursera duke or ucsd python for everybody univ of michigan coursera data structures algorithms algorithms specialization stanford coursera data structures and algorithms nanodegree udacity leetcode hackerrank practice also strengthens your technical profile computer systems operating systems nand tetris building a modern computer from first principles coursera operating systems and you becoming a power user google coursera databases software engineering databases specialization stanford edx software engineering essentials alberta coursera math quantitative readiness discrete mathematics discrete math for computer science coursera uc san diego national research univ linear algebra probability statistics linear algebra for machine learning coursera imperial college probability and statistics for engineers edx tu delft mitx calculus refresher if needed single variable calculus mit opencourseware professional certificates strong for applications r sum google it automation with python coursera industry recognized practical ibm applied data science certificate coursera strong mix of python sql data tools mitx micromasters in statistics and data science edx graduate level rigor great prep harvardx professional certificate in computer science for python lightweight but brand name', 'edx website harvard s cs series harvard university s online computer science classes legitimate harvard university classes all free to access the course or dollars if you want to order a certificate at the end and you are allowed to use the course content for your students as well cs x their general online cs course that is a crash course in several different programming language basics cs s a very small starter course that is good to take before cs s that is about working with scratch it shows you all about how to work with scratch and it gives you a boost on your first problem set on cs x which is making a scratch project cs p a crash course in working with python cs ai a crash course in ai there are also courses in working with c and javascript gaming mobile apps and computer science for business and law r cs', 'if you want the biggest return on your time investment towards building a solid foundation you can leverage app academy open preferred and or full stack open the reason for starting here is to get a fast paced rigorous introduction by throwing yourself into a bootcamp style program this will give you a broad overview plus a practical introduction to building things common developer tools and plenty of hands on practice cs cs web programming cs ai and cs mobile you may not be able to afford tuition for an ivy league university right now but harvard will give you a proper cs introduction and fundamentals for web ai and mobile dev that will help you develop a breadth of understanding across a spectrum of career possibilities with which you can focus your future learning eloquent javascript and think python to make sure you have a good foundation in more than one language and have dived into them deep enough that you know more than the few snippets you learned off stack overflow when you got stumped practice with frontend mentor projects and code signal and hackerrank problems because practice is crucial while you re on code signal make special note of their interview practice section it gives an extremely good walk through of each common data structure and algorithm get creative with your frontend mentor practice use the projects as a rough idea to build out full stack mobile or desktop apps then assess where you are what you enjoy and where you d like to devote additional time options include khan academy or mitocw to build a bit more of a math foundation if you find that the problems you enjoy tackling would be easier to tackle with a better understanding of math mitocw has a couple fantastic algorithms courses the mongodb team puts out excellent nosql database courses if you want to dive deeper into frontend hit up the official docs for angular react and vue and see which appeals most to you then study tf out of it study testing a bit udacity has a good course every single book recommended by teachyourselfcs is excellent actively studying any of them would be time well spent not free read the pragmatic programmer it s pure gold not free consider what open source projects align with your learning goals and begin contributing you ll learn a lot plus get valuable experience notes given your stipulations don t waste your time on freecodecamp there are some wonderful people in the community but the actual curriculum is intentionally kept blindingly easy to keep people motivated a focused learner would be better served with a more rigorous curriculum the odin project is great but app academy open tackles the same content at a more rigorous level if you re not afraid of working harder go for aao you ll learn more this is not a you re guaranteed to get a job list that list doesn t exist and anyone who s peddling guarantees is a snake oil salesman this is a list that if followed with serious effort and study will help you develop the foundation necessary to forge your own path towards whatever dev career your interests and talents steer you to pursue']

Name: 172_tensorflow_tensor_library_tensor flow
Representative_Docs: ['creating your own machine learning library in c can be a rewarding and educational experience allowing you to deepen your understanding of the intricacies of machine learning algorithms here are the steps you can take to embark on this journey define the scope clearly define the scope of your machine learning library decide which algorithms and functionalities you want to implement research algorithms deepen your knowledge of machine learning algorithms understand the mathematics and logic behind each algorithm you plan to include design the api design a clean and intuitive application programming interface api for your library think about how users will interact with and integrate your library into their projects start small begin by implementing simpler algorithms to build a solid foundation gradually progress to more complex ones code responsibly write clean modular and well documented code this will make your library easier to understand use and maintain optimization optimize your code for performance consider efficient data structures and algorithms to make your library faster testing implement a comprehensive testing framework to ensure the correctness of your library test various scenarios and edge cases documentation provide thorough documentation explain the purpose usage and inner workings of each component this will make it easier for others and yourself to use the library open source collaboration consider making your library open source collaboration with the community can lead to valuable feedback and improvements frulow integration to share your library with a wider audience consider posting it on frulow frulow is a platform designed for developers to showcase and discover libraries efficiently by sharing your machine learning library on frulow you can reach more users in less time fostering collaboration and making your contribution more accessible to the developer community remember creating a machine learning library is a substantial undertaking so take it step by step learn as you go and enjoy the process of building something valuable for yourself and others', 'i tried the google course they throw you into tensorflow right away that s horrible imo i didn t get any of the coding answers right and had to do the reveal answer for all but the most basic coding questions i wasn t learning anything because of this so i had to stop tensorflow is a daunting platform for someone who has never been exposed to anything in machine learning and also even if you have had prior exposure to ml like i have personally i am not comfortable at all with tensorflow i know that i ll eventually have to learn to use it but as the very first ml platform you re exposed to that ll put you off ml for a long time i don t know why they throw you into tensorflow instead of letting you write some basic machine learning helper functions in standard python the way andrew ng does on coursera he actually starts with matlab octave then you go into python in later courses i still struggle with what a tensor is and all the functions involved in calling tensors i don t want to learn that at the same time i m learning basic concepts of machine learning that should be something you learn after you have all the ml basics down', 'i was asked to teach a course about a new version deep learning framework by google tensorflow version to a company of highly technical people creating smart acoustic and microphone systems for recording studios tensorflow came out during the middle of and is very different than the previous version so there was a lot of learning to do and trial and error in the face of unknown code api and bugs this took me about months of almost full time job to complete after the course finished i also asked the students to fill in a review about it so i will have their input during the process i learned a lot about learning creative work and presentation i am sharing this here both for my own sake and for anyone else who might benefit from this knowledge you can also read the post in my blog how to learn new material map information sources go online and filter and map all quality sources of material when the subject is quite new there are not so many quality information sources online doing a first map of all the good quality sources you found gives access to information and at the same time confidence that you are not going to miss out on information because you didn t know of another source for example these sources can be youtube videos official documentation open source side projects books online lectures conferences events which have a written online record of what was taught stack overflow questions comments there are sources of sources online pages that reference to other quality information sources a sort of dynamic online index these can be reddit posts youtube lectures with references by the google tensorflow team in my case good github pages and more go over all of them and map the most relevant and good information sources you will use also possible to ask friends for reference materials in my case the subject was so new almost non of my tech friends could help while you do this first initial mapping you will inevitably encounter the most significant topics of to learn and teach start writing down recurring topic names chapter or topics which you saw might be extra interesting or relevant even though they are not the main topics you will also encounter knowledgeable people or specific quality sources check all their content you might find more missing gems there mark specific references which you might use in whole or part while teaching either as teaching material or exercise material in my tensorflow learning i found these resources to be extremely helpful google tensorflow guides and tutorials everything by aur lien g ron book stanford class tensorflow youtube channel especially with the inside tensorflow playlist and videos from tf conferences these reddit posts this github page with a huge amount of resources and references filtering briefly check each information source to see if it is good or just someone posting content more for self promotion marketing and less for giving real value and insight info about a topic make sure you only have the best reference for each sub topic if a subtopic has more than information sources save all of them use only the best main and if you need extra info then you have where to look while you are filtering you will start to learn the basic concepts of what you are learning you will start to get a feel for what you should focus more on later what is extra interesting going over tensorflow materials i found that a lot of the materials might say they are for tensorflow but actually they are for tensorflow also a lot of the materials were brief and shallow explanations without real proper content and value this filtering step took me about two days to finish first reading white papers architecture reviews read thoroughly i started off my tensorflow learning process by reading the google whitepaper from for the framework interestingly i also finished learning tensorflow with this white paper after i went over all the other material and presentations when i started learning the white paper helped me understand which important interesting complicated topics i will encounter and what i should teach i ended with the white paper because it had in depth explanation about the specifics of the framework which were hard to find in other places official documentation skim the purpose is check what will be hard and tricky to learn which means it will consume a lot of time and effort and what will be easy check what are the most important things you should focus on first what are the basics of the main subject which all other sub topics depend on do at least one significant non trivial tutorial to start feeling the topic better and raising more questions in depth reading once you have listed in order the important things to focus one start going over them one by one using the references which you found this stage can be unexpectedly long and hard but it is important for your overall familiarity of what you are learning and for your confidence in what you learned do not skip and or rush it write down short summaries and the most significant sub topics you encounter along with links to the reference materials where you learned of these sub topics this will be the basis of what you will be teaching later if you find a specific sub topic is taking you longer then expected or maybe the sources you find don t cover it well this is a good sign it means you have found an area where you can learn something new not many people online know and you can teach it later if it is an immediately important sub topic focus on it understand it if required read the open source until you familiarize yourself with the topic take notes during your learning this will be your source of information and your audience s if this is not immediately important write this subtopic down in your open items and make sure you have a task to come back and research this subtopic later while you are learning you will encounter questions and things you don t understand if it is part of your learning flow go ahead and explore them if not write the open questions you have in your notes and continue learning the main thing you set out to learn these questions are important your real understanding comes from asking these questions which means you understand what you are learning and you try to transfer it to something new that wasn t completely explained also when later you will answer these question the search for the answer and the final answer will greatly enlarge your overall understanding and confidence in your own understanding check back on previously opened questions which might be related to a subtopic you are now learning most chances you will be able to answer it if you see you are still stuck even after the depth reading this might be related to point above or you might need to learn another sub topic before you could answer the question practice and recall what you learned try out things by yourself your own code based on what you learned see if they work or not try out special use cases or or esoteric implementations which you thought of but are not written in your references this will both help you remember the subject better and will raise issues you are not sure about in your knowledge also this could be good class examples or exercises later while you are learning if you encounter open questions people posted which you find answers to post the answers online it will help your own understanding will help others and will promote you points can be executed simultaneously interleaving them until you cover all the interesting references point needs to be handled by itself in a focused manner summarize log or write down things you are not sure of later when you learn more deeply you can delete these summaries or change them according to your new found knowledge you can mark things you are unsure of i use triple question marks also writing it down allows you to think it through understand better and remember what you learned even if partially don t be afraid to waste your time writing down something you might delete later bugs in the framework can be very frustrating and time consuming when a code is not working as expected make sure it is not working first by minimizing it as much as you can and checking the documentation if it should be working then search google and github to see if anyone else wrote anything about it if there is nothing written you will need to double check yourself to make sure it is a bug most of my personal tensorflow learning was through encountering bugs when i was trying to run my own code from having the class ask me in depth questions and from just experimenting with different graph structures and different ways to write similar functionalities for example everything i was running in eager mode i tried also running in graph mode and vice versa one of the trickiest part to learn was to try and understand what goes under the hood with tf data i used the inside tensorflow youtube video about it to great lengths one of the things that wasted a lot of my time was a bug with tf which was released while my course was running and tensorboard it wasn t profiling gpu usage took me a full day to accept that this is a bug and not something wrong which i am doing and an extra day and cloud server charges to setup a working environment with tf instead of tf x b what i learned about preparing presentations for each topic the cool thing is that if the learning part was done right this next part is kind of straightforward you already have most of the materials you want to talk about and present you also have references to interesting things to show in class and you have a breakdown of the information dependencies between the different subtopics you also already went through many presentation on the same topics so you can follow their lines if you saw the presentations your good along with powerpoint presentations i use jupyter notebook to run python code in class the jupyter notebook allows to show how code runs in real time when exceptions occur and why and also allows an interactive way of teaching in which you can challenge the class with questions about how specific code will run and see the results live guidelines put all your summaries and reference materials infront of you in a notebook or in powerpoint add any other points you think are relevant for the presentation at this stage you will see how about how long a presentation for one topic will take separate all the material to presentation powerpoint slides presentation colab jupyter notebook code exercise bonus information practice fully create the presentation slides write notes to yourself which you will use in class especially write notes if you are going to present something that connect to something later on in the presentation explain every keyword you talk about fully create jupyter code notebooks try to keep text to a minimum but enough of it so that you will remember what to talk about make sure all dependencies are installed as part of the notebook execution flow good both for teaching about dependencies and making sure your code will run on any machine separate the topic into sections help organize thoughts and manage time while presenting maybe you will want to skip some sections if you run out of time along with the jupyter notebook you should have a small reference notebooks with things you should talk about in class while presenting the notebook repeat until you feel that you covered everything for the topic you wanted points should be done separately to points points require imagination abstract thinking and experimentation if you combine it with the technical details of creating and editing the presentation you will get frustrated and won t be able to do an effective job while you build your presentation you might find out you are missing knowledge about a specific sub topic go back to the in depth part above and research into that topic create buffer material which you will teach if there is extra time your original time estimates will never be completely accurate if you run out of time you can start off the next class from where you stopped if you finish before time the extra buffer material will help you fill that void x b what i learned about presenting the focus should be on having people understand what you are teaching not in covering all the materials it is ok and advised to skip miss parts if it means people will understand what you teach better take pauses when presenting laugh with the audience tell stories actively think in advance what stories you will during the presentation it both fills time gives rest and fun along with regular break times add mini breaks during your presentation in these mini breaks your talk or show something cool which relates to the subject being taught this both helps to refresh and interest people in what you are teaching further for example i showed cool research videos of the latest things in deep learning and deep fake later in the reviews the students said this was a cool addition to the course if something interests you it will interest the audience both because it is an interesting topic and because of your personal interest and excitement about making perfect presentation error less interesting and smooth the audience is extremely focused in trying to grasp and understand new concepts they don t notice the imperfections in your presentation they notice the imperfections in their understanding of the material don t worry about making it perfect worry about making it and making it interesting for you while putting as much effort as you can in bringing the audience value in my course i thought i did a poor job with explaining everything but the students told me that the tools i gave them will be very valuable for their work and the code samples were very interesting for them x b about time management in face of unknown materials or task when you start working on it there are many things you are not familiar with many things that can suck up your time without you being prepared some guidelines on how to manage that briefly skim the material you need to learn present beforehand to have a rough estimate on how complicated it is this is why the first stage of mapping your data sources when learning is so important if there are many tutorials and explanations about a subject you can estimate time quite accurately the risk is covering subjects which don t have a lot of easily accessible information these usually consume most of the time it is sometimes scary or demotivating to work only to find out you have much more work than expected to handle that keep a very elaborate task list with priorities and always focus on the small next step just on what s the most important next small thing start gaining small wins these will help you get the motivation to cover an unexpectedly large topic if you catch yourself studying deeply into a subject that might not be so important stop write it down to check later and address the next subject on the list you might find a subject is not so important only after you dive into it and find out it is raw buggy or not the main focus of what you are studying teaching always remember you don t have to know all the exact details of everything you need to know enough to get by and enough to teach your class if you are stuck and can t progress a helpful tip is to write down a question for yourself why am i stuck and answer it with atleast different answers you will find out exactly what s blocking your path and will be able to tackle it head on and unstuck yourself x b archiving and references you should use a notebook or other method of writing and archiving for summaries of important or at first seemingly important subtopics and subjects with reference to where you learned it reference materials ideas to explore open questions tasks i used evernote notes resources all the references i found and my initial raw ideas tasks the next tasks i should handle and any open items unclear things i want to learn lecture the structure of what i will present the subtopics main concepts exercises and bonus references for class']

Name: 177_glean_transcription_recording_notes
Representative_Docs: ['i ve always had students who were allowed to audio record lectures as an accommodation no big deal of course they re not allowed to share the recordings post them online etc because that s the professor s work not the student s and the professor controls the intellectual property i ve never had a student to my knowledge break this rule this year my university started signing up students for glean which allows them to audio record lectures but also uploads the audio to glean servers and encourages students to upload your slides and other visuals it makes no guarantees about how it will be used and who owns the content it auto transcribes them and uses the text to train ai wtf i didn t consent to have my lectures taken and used by this company and i certainly don t consent to my work being used to train ai also i m not aware of any evidence that this software actually improves disabled students learning research shows that audio recording generally has little to no effect students rarely re listen they don t pay full attention during class because they know they have a backup they don t pay full attention when re listening if they actually do whatever makes it hard for them to process lecture is still in place when they re listen etc i just didn t care because it had no real downside but if we re being asked to implement something that has very serious downsides i would like to see evidence that it produces superior outcomes', 'i m sorry to grave dig this thread but as a student with disabilities i do agree with you on some of the tone in some of these messages while i understand the concerns with ai i have always hated how faculty push back on recording accommodations because of intellectual property concerns as someone who needs recording assistance to participate in the classroom the last thing on my mind is selling your lectures that said as a student with adhd and problems with auditory processing i hate glean my undergrad used sonocent which while it had flaws like storing recordings on the device in huge gigabyte files and didn t have transcription it allowed relatively robust native note taking i could highlight text and audio in different colors have multiple levels of bullet points and indenting bold and underline and move the beginning and end of audio segments to match the text glean has almost none of those functions with sonocent i could take my notes and use the audio for parts i couldn t understand or zoned out for with glean i have to take my notes elsewhere and am just left with a raw recording to parse through for parts i missed because the native note taking elements would barely be sufficient for a high schooler let alone grad school you also cant change what part of audio text is attached to for hour grad school classes i just don t have time to manage a recording that long unless i can also take written notes well within the program it s clear glean was built by tech bros with no consultation with people with disabilities or expertise or training in pedagogy for people who learn differently due to neurodivergence', 'hello i m david from glean s support team and i wanted to jump in and try to answer some of your questions and address some of your concerns about what glean is actually doing with your data i hope that s ok i do understand the concerns over how ai is going to affect education and especially how your data and ip is wrapped up in that whilst i understand this reply won t address your concerns around ai i can hopefully give you some reassurance about how glean is actually using your data firstly we don t currently have any ai features within glean we do have transcription and live caption features which might be where the confusion lies for transcription we do send a small sample of audio files to our third party provider so that we can benchmark transcription accuracy against other providers and we are transparent about this in our privacy policy and in our faculty faqs but the data is only being used to improve the quality of the text being generated it is not being shared with third party ai providers in order to train their commercial language models although we do use aws to store data we do not use their transcription services and we re going to be moving to a new transcription provider soon which will mean we do not even send the data mentioned above i also wanted to add that we do not rent or sell any of our users data including the audio being captured either in a raw or aggregated way we simply use the data to make glean better for our users as some in the thread have said simply recording audio is not always a big benefit to students however giving students the ability to quickly and easily make their own notes against recorded audio is a massive benefit and that s how glean helps your students learn more from your classes if you want to try it for yourself just to see how it works then you could either sign up to a free trial or get in touch with us we would be happy to take you through a demo and answer your questions we have been supporting students since with our older applications and now glean so we ve got a lot of experience on how students really do benefit from our tools we really are here to try and help your students succeed not to sell data i d appreciate it if you could take a few minutes to read some of our white papers especially the latest where we asked real students about using glean and how it affected their grades you can find that at we have heard you so we will be doing more on our faculty pages to better address your concerns about how your data is actually being used by glean we ve got some new faq s being released in the next week if you have any questions about glean then please get in touch we d love to hear from you even if it s negative feedback that s how we can improve and if anyone would like to help us get the faculty information on our website right for you then please reach out we d really like to work with you on this']

Name: 178_cheating_cheat_students_cheaters
Representative_Docs: ['when i was first entering academia the general mindset was that cheating was a symptom of bad teaching that the average student cheated because they felt overwhelmed and unsupported sure there were a few genuinely lazy students but most cheating could be addressed through better instruction i ve met similar comments outside of academia too i think ai has absolutely disrupted this viewpoint in the past cheating was pretty easy to prove if you could be caught especially with software like turnitin now students feel like ai can t be proven they think they can t actually be punished for cheating so we re seeing cheating everywhere while there are several other circumstances influences this i think this is the primary difference in the numbers of cheaters i saw pre and post chatgpt the gap in this time too small to be caused only by social differences i often wonder how this has impacted our general understanding of cheating i think a lot of the old philosophy failed to take into account the perceived risks of cheating which would have been much higher in the past', 'my answer today on this question will be different than my answer tomorrow on this question but here s my today answer part one there will always be cheaters there have always been cheaters there will always be cheaters the majority of students are not cheaters if they cheat it s often out of having made a dumb decision fear than malice are we teaching to the cheaters no they are there and we sometimes have to deal with them but they are not our target audience we need to stop teaching to the bottom start teaching to the middle top part two students are not using ai in the way that we think this semester i included a mandatory ai use statement for each of my units the results from their responses were incredibly eye opening from my students the following trends emerged of students are never ai ers and not in a kiss ass way of the students who shared ai use the trends were as follows a a concern about diction syntax basically worried they don t sound smart enough b anxiety over not understanding the prompt used ai to soothe anxiety make sure their idea fit the prompt in essence most of the ai use came about because the kids were scared this is an opportunity for us to think about our pedagogy namely in how we can offer other non ai sources as tools to alleviate anxiety as an aside the kid in your example who printed off all the potential prompts he could think of is kind of hilarious think of all the prompt work he had to do to end up carting in a usable stack of essay responses how is that different than the kid to uses the most minuscule writing to cram every note onto the cheat sheet i ll probably be cranky about student ai use tomorrow but today i m thinking that we don t really understand or embrace how students are using this technology and that lack of understanding is to our own detriment', 'i get the argument students have been cheating since time immemorial to be sure however no student is obliged to be dishonest in their ai use just like no student is obliged to cheat peers have always seen their colleagues in class cheating they ve always heard about students cheating but they chose not to cheat because of potential penalties and to maintain their moral compass not even for the altruistic goal of actually learning something i do see a sliver of students using ai and i call them out i haven t had anyone really go to the mat in defending their use of ai while insisting that they didn t at least not yet i suspect that is the next thing i am going to see since i read so many accounts of precisely that here on this subreddit however aversion is still a reasonable course to plot university administrators however have spent the last several decades supporting the notion that education is a consumer not a public good that is purchased by the student for their own use as they see fit that is probably why i ve read so many accounts of universities now putting the burden on faculty or simply embracing which means different things to different institutions the use of ai here s the rub for me ai can already complete traditional assignments better than most people i couldn t disagree more quicker absolutely using reasonable logic to construct answers yeah easier to cheat you betcha but better than most people nah ai output is vanilla non creative garbage that reads like a wikipedia entry it s not that difficult to id i know the online ai checkers aren t perfect but they re pretty good universities should see this as an existential threat to their existence rather than another technology that they must embrace because students are embracing it this is kinda like a public university calling for an end to governmental financial aid basically punching themselves in the face because it might be entertaining for their customers and earn them a few more tuition dollars i ve never stopped using pencil and paper scantron exams in class for my introductory courses in my upper division courses exams are blue book and handwritten for the larger written work i ve only had a couple of instances where majors had the temerity to present ai output as their own work i suspect the student population will get bolder but instructors will still be able to id ai output in fact i think we ll get better at it going forward the bottom line is that if an instructor prohibits ai use and the student feels like being dishonest is mandatory then that s on the student we cannot reasonably be held responsible for student dishonesty']

Name: 180_distributed_learning engineer_engineer_distributed systems
Representative_Docs: ['in projects just play around with the openai or claude api and try to get something to solve some known problem with that to reproduce my experience like years into ml and data science the long path is as follows learn probability theory calculus discrete math proofs and linear algebra do a lot of analysis projects where your role is to basically find interesting insights in data or to quantify uncertainty ship features where the algorithm delivering the feature is probabilistic more than ml or llm you need to gain an intuition about how you build products when outputs are probabilistic how you prove things will work how to quantify success et cetera unless you study a narrow niche of ml and get one of the few academic bent industry jobs the ai money is all in product development to do that you either join a big company and get randomly assigned to a team like that or have enough experience using emerging tech that you can make a solid case to be on that team imo doing something like ai right now is more similar to working in any emerging tech then it is classical ml per se i work on ai products now at a big company and doing that is much more influential than knowing any specific topic', 'i have around years of experience and have been getting some interviews ive been failing them though been doing a little bit better than how i used to do before but the bar has gone up drastically since i had to really look for a job about years ago the vast majority of jobs i m getting are related to ai and their data pipeline i ve been just buildling out restful apis so not really caught up in ai at all outside of the mainstream generative chatgpt news i ve started ramping up on langchain might drop this for pydantic vector dbs and how llms work my understanding is that many companies out there are just chaining together llms writing some workers apis to store a ton of data for their models and ultimately process it and store it on some vector db if they want to do some queries against it basically a lot of the math that has made ds ml hard to get into has been abstracted into libraries please correct me if any of my assumptions are wrong above do i need to know a lot of the math i took multi calc many years ago and remember stopping at linear alg because i found out my major didn t need it so i dropped the class i looked up some linear alg and understand why matrix math is so important but can t derive anything and have not done any problems whatsoever is prompt engineering really that important it looks like you re just writing templates for system message and managing the context memory i assume this is more of an art and is probably something you make configureable and expose as a ui for the product person to test what else should i ramp up on to make myself market ready would love books or topics to look into', 'i had very little distributed system design exposure my experience was working full stack on controller apps for smart appliances frameworks for augmented reality hardware writing firmware and userspace daemons to coordinate sensor data processing reinforcement learning research for cognitive assessments designing training and deploying models plus data analysis identifying malicious requests in web application firewalls plus provisioning deprovisioning containers to run that logic ai for detecting regressions in web applications before shipping new versions that series of jobs gave me a unique skill set but only incidental exposure to system design when integrating my work into an existing distributed system that s why my system design skills weren t sufficient to have a chance without heavy preparation the team that wants me needs those specific skills operating system programming augmented reality hardware and productionalizing neural networks it won t require designing distributed systems but all high level roles must demonstrate those design skills in the onsite interviews regardless this requirement isn t a negative thing since anything we make becomes part of a distributed system at some point and learning having skills makes you understand the system on a deeper level to do the job better']

when i was about to create a bad solution and everything in between it gives me project ideas it taught me golang it lets me feel like a proficient python developer despite the fact that i spend results you want but i can t comprehend how anyone thinks it s overrated it s a literal game changer', 'gt write a reddit post worried about chatgpt taking programming jobs away gt gt gt hey everyone gt gt i m a little worried about chatgpt and other large language models like it potentially taking programming jobs away from us these models are getting pretty good at generating code and i m our jobs easier and more efficient but i can t help but feel a little uneasy about it i mean we ve already seen how automation has impacted other industries and i don t want to see the same thing happen to software engineering gt gt what do you guys think is chatgpt and other language models something we should be worried about or am i just being paranoid how do you see these models fitting is like the reason people perform poorly usually has nothing to do with how fast they code it has to do with understanding the architecture deeply enough to know which part of the massive package to Name: 182_accounting_accountants_bookkeeping_automated
Representative_Docs: ['accounting has always been an area ripe for outsourcing traditional automation and now ai tasks like bookkeeping invoice processing data entry reconciliation and sample audits using ai ml have steadily become more automated over time in many companies it departments still report to the cfo because finance and accounting were often the first areas where businesses introduced computers to automate work and as always if you can t automate it you outsource it there are literally hundreds of thousands of people in the philipines who are outsourced contractors doing accounting review for big companies most people are now familiar with the copilot model of ai you don t replace entire departments but instead more senior team members have boosted productivity and don t need juniors to do grunt work anymore even if that s where they would normally earn their chops accounting feels especially susceptible to this model even more than programming a cpa and years of experience are still valuable but the edge coming in without experience is shrinking even if ai doesn t continue to improve at its current pace and entry level accounting positions are underpaid which makes it less attractive in general rule based automation for structured data has been around for a while but recent ai advancements have made it possible to automate tasks involving unstructured data like documents for example let s say businesses communicate internally through tools like slack salesforce and zendesk as a side note there are ai and search tools gaining popularity that can index and search that data to act as a knowledge store with references links to sources you ask it questions off your internal documentation and that s an ai use case that s legitimately working pretty well but externally companies mostly communicate with each other through documents contracts pdfs invoices purchase orders order forms etc and yeah of course less structured stuff too like e mails these documents require legal review but there s also a lot of manual accounting review work involved to look at the financial implications of different contracts track revenue report on it and audit the process now ai tools that can scan documents and extract or flag specific types of information are becoming more common converting unstructured data into structured data for easier review with a high level of consistency and the ability to reference sources no one actually likes doing this review but it s pretty manual work stuff like invoices are generally not super complicated and there s still going to be some manual review work involved but it s just one area task in the accounting field where ai does have a legitimate boost to efficiency and a lot of cfos caos controllers and director of accounting folks love this stuff of course there will still be high trust high skill areas forensic accounting full audit prep and execution complex tax strategy interpreting financial data where a real accounting background remains essential and you wouldn t trust that to an ai but there s a lot of areas where ai has an opportunity to carve out work the open question remains about the quality of implementation the level of trust in the system speed cost and if even ai is worth it over just outsourcing', 'accounting will be replaced with ai', 'never heard of that phrase before do you mean that accounting is going to be taken over by ai software as opposed to people working the jobs people think accounting is only limited to accounts payable accounts receivable etc its actually not accounting has a lot of different routes you can take audit tax business consulting finance it forensics research teaching etc i knew someone who went into investment banking i can never see it as being an automated field due to its complexity in nature and if people say that to you they re on some high horse until you are actually in the accounting field mainly public accounting firms it s hard to see the different types of accounting jobs careers paths that are out there so i can see why some people may think the field can get automated because they re not exposed to the different types of accounting and only have the purview of lower level accounting work']

Name: 183_embedded_software_career_technical
Representative_Docs: ['context my career has been in digital banking fortune as a accredited project manager in uk business side and front end technical delivery i m currently at mid management level not deal breakers i d like to use my existing experience and build on my technical ability i don t have a technical programming background problem solving grasping complex problems and explain them i laymen terms are a given moving forward i find cyber security machine learning broadly ai enterprise architecture are areas that peak my interest at the moment i ve taken free cs lectures by mit which again interested me my previous stint in college in programming meant i didn t have the attention span for it taking this all into account there s significant scope in the new direction and i m open for success in practical terms rather than academically i m reluctant to take on a cert until i m sure the particularly area of study is where i can add value or is a self study course best any strategic consulting presentations and powerpoint basically everything i don t enjoy i recently quit this position i now managed to land a technical role as an embedded software engineer at a large multinational aerospace and defense company even though embedded development isn t directly aligned with my studies i really enjoy programming problem solving and low level technical challenges so i m genuinely excited about the new role however i found out that the tech stack relies heavily on c and ada which at least from my perspective seem somewhat outdated my main reasons for accepting this role were escaping traditional strategic consulting like accenture s even though technically it s still consulting body rental at least now i ll focus on one specific technical project instead of juggling multiple non technical tasks the company offers strong international mobility opportunities europe asia usa which align closely with my personal and professional priorities my longer term goals aren t completely clear yet i initially thought i d stay in academia and research ml but now i m more inclined toward working on low level latency sensitive projects ideally using innovative technologies in c or rust i m also quite interested in quantitative finance or joining big tech companies primarily due to their innovation given my math and ml background roles involving machine learning or deep learning also seem appealing i d also love to explore high performance systems programming or low level ai infrastructure linux kernel dev robotics or high frequency trading infrastructure among other things however i m not sure how easy it ll be to pivot from ada c embedded roles into such fields i d prefer avoiding anything frontend or web development related in my free time i m actively studying c and rust deepening my knowledge of ml frameworks i ve previously used at university tensorflow pytorch and contributing to open source projects though my free time is currently limited i ve considered pursuing certifications but i m not sure they re valuable enough on a cv given this context my main questions for you are would you recommend sticking to embedded software c ada for at least years before trying to pivot into a more modern software engineering field e g c rust or ml infrastructure or should i aim to switch sooner are there examples of people successfully moving from ada c embedded roles into fields like linux kernel development robotics rust systems development or similar areas is my fear of being stuck justified or will my embedded experience still be highly valued and easily transferable any advice experiences or insights would be greatly appreciated thanks', 'me after the conversation can you make a tl dr of our conversation related to the career advice you have given chatgpt sure here is a tl dr of the career advice i provided if you re looking for a career that allows you to use your skills in software engineering as part of a broader role that involves interacting with customers and working on product development there are a few different paths you could consider such as working as a product manager or engineer working as a consultant or contractor for small businesses or starting your own business being unemployed for an extended period of time can make it more difficult to find a job so it might be helpful to focus on building your skills and experience through internships freelance work or other opportunities in order to make yourself more attractive to potential employers it s important to carefully consider your skills and interests and choose a path that allows you to use your strengths and pursue your passions with time and effort you will be able to find a job that allows you to make a positive impact me i have searched for software engineering work but i don t find it to be compelling on it own i enjoy software and tech to be part of the work but not all of the work so i would like to work on product and with customers as well i have thought about to be a freelancer making websites for small businesses is this a good idea or should i keep searching for a software engineering work in a startup instead chatgpt it sounds like you re looking for a career that allows you to use your skills in software engineering as part of a broader role that involves interacting with customers and working on product development freelancing as a web developer for small businesses could be a good way to achieve this as it would give you the opportunity to use your technical skills to create websites for customers while also allowing you to interact directly with those customers and work on developing products that meet their needs working in a startup as a software engineer could also be a good option as it would give you the opportunity to be part of a small dynamic team where you would have the opportunity to work on a wide range of projects and be involved in the entire product development process this could be particularly appealing if you enjoy working on cutting edge technology and solving complex problems ultimately the right choice for you will depend on your personal preferences and goals if you re interested in working on a variety of projects and being involved in the entire product development process a startup might be a good fit for you if you prefer to have more control over your work and the projects you take on freelancing could be a good option it s important to carefully consider your goals and priorities and choose the option that best aligns with them me is there something else i could work on to achieve the career you described chatgpt if you re looking for a career that allows you to use your skills in software engineering as part of a broader role that involves interacting with customers and working on product development there are a few different paths you could consider one option is to look for a job as a product manager or product engineer at a technology company in this role you would be responsible for working closely with customers to understand their needs and develop products that meet those needs you would also work with a team of engineers to design build and test the product and you would be involved in all aspects of the product development process another option is to work as a consultant or contractor for small businesses in this role you would be responsible for providing technical expertise and support to small businesses as they develop and implement new technology solutions you would work closely with the business owners to understand their needs and develop solutions that meet those needs and you would be involved in all aspects of the project from planning and design to implementation and testing finally you could consider starting your own business this would give you the opportunity to use your skills and expertise to create products and services that meet the needs of your customers and you would have the freedom to determine your own work schedule and the projects you take on starting a business can be challenging but it can also be very rewarding and allow you to build a career that is truly your own ultimately the right path for you will depend on your personal preferences and goals it s important to carefully consider your skills and interests and choose a path that allows you to use your strengths and pursue your passions me would the fact that i m unemployed and have been looking for work months change your take on this chatgpt the fact that you are currently unemployed and have been looking for work for two months does not change the overall advice i provided however it is important to consider the fact that being unemployed for an extended period of time can make it more difficult to find a job in this situation it might be helpful to focus on building your skills and experience through internships freelance work or other opportunities in order to make yourself more attractive to potential employers this could also help you gain practical experience and build a portfolio of work that you can show to potential employers additionally it might be helpful to network with other professionals in your field and reach out to potential employers directly to express your interest in working for their organization this can help you connect with potential employers and learn about job opportunities that may not be advertised elsewhere overall it s important to remain persistent and focused on your goals and to continue pursuing opportunities that align with your interests and skills with time and effort you will be able to find a job that allows you to use your skills and experience to make a positive impact']

Name: 184_python_packages_matlab_pandas
Representative_Docs: ['in my experience biologists tended to use excel matlab r or python depending on their background and research area the informatics types leaned toward programming chemists and material scientists i ve met have used excel matlab python r or julia for figures engineers bio ee chemical and others were similar to chemists computer scientists have mostly used python or julia but they have mostly been working in machine learning statisticians my specialty typically use r for their graphs within r they use ggplot mostly but bayesians also use other tools younger professors and grad students are gravitating to python as well to use the ml libraries there but that s unimportant for you at the moment i d suspect i can t speak for psychologists or mathematicians or geologists or social scientists i d imagine they are similar except math folks will sometimes use a latex package called tikz for their figures very painful imo but to each their own everyone i m referring to was either a grad student postdoc or professor undergrads in statistics are taught r typically i can t speak for other disciplines it is very common to export graphics from those languages as svg graphics and finish touching them up using inkscape or adobe illustrator photoshop photoshop for raster graphics of e g spatial models r and python are both pretty accessible if all you want to do is plot your data r is easiest to get started with if you just download rstudio and follow their instructions to get started youtube has a lot of info on getting set up and plotting and there s a site called datacamp that provides very basic tutorials to learn r or python textbook graphics are usually adapted from figures made by researchers in the fields mentioned the author or publisher may hire an artist or graphic designer to finish off the formatting if it s an intro book in some fields i ve read one or two books where this was definitely the case although i d consider it an exception not a norm in academic publishing where it is most likely to occur is with freshman books that will be used at several universities like an algebra or calculus text', 'years ago my professors and colleagues all said r that s changed very rapidly even my elderly professor who looks like he s decided to learn python i finally got around to learning r and python properly and realized i can do matlab and r functions inside python python has become a jack of all trades it s so versatile it s really gonna be here to stay edit some things are easier and faster to write in r than in python sure that comment applies to lots of languages and the speed of those languages will be faster yes it s true but when computers came out and ppl said it s faster to use a regular calculator than a computer sure i guess it kinda was but now i can google math equations and it will calculate it along with the symbols and convert units and currency that s kinda what it s like with python vs insert whatever language yes r is better for stats and matlab is better for graphs and c is faster but python is so universal that i can do things all those languages can do and things that they can t do at all you can reach down to the easy calculator functions and you can reach up to programming a robot with a custom built ai voice assistant that can understand your voice commands it s seriously the difference between a calculator and a computer python can easily work with csv files probably you ll want to use the pandas library for that i m so glad to see someone else using imagej i never played around with programming in it but i would expect python would be that flexibility you re looking for and it can definitely handle the visualization parts as well really it s likely to be calling from the matplotlib library in that case so really you ll also be learning to use matlab thru python but for free', 'as many others in this thread have said python is a great multipurpose language however i feel like r has been unfairly maligned i work in data science and i use both r and python as complementary tools here s my take r has a larger and higher quality package ecosystem than python when it comes to statistics if you re looking for some arcane estimator chances are it s been implemented in r for data wrangling r is much more intuitive and less verbose than python i know people have been singing the scipy stack s praises here but working with tidyverse packages in r is a few order of magnitudes better than working with raw numpy and pandas there s a lot of weird stuff you need to be wary of in python that doesn t take up mental space in r rstudio is the best data science ide period the standard for data visualisation in r ggplot is leagues ahead of matplotlib contrary to popular belief r is better for reporting or at least equal rmarkdown is a bit like a jupyter notebook but you can produce data driven briefings presentations whatever this is great for business now i think for the more serious complaint people have with r r is often called ugly inconsistent not a proper programming language but rather a statistical tool its object system s are a joke programmers often hate it and i did too for these reasons until i realised that r is a functional language it s not object oriented at its core you can write more elegant concise and meaningful code in r than you can in python if you accept the fact that you ll need to be a functional programmer most programmers are not which i think explains at least some of the dislike however learning and treating r as a functional language will make you happier and improve your code read up on functional programming with that in mind note the downsides r is actually incredibly inconsistent while the tidyverse goes some way to remedy this you ll still find three mutually exclusive object systems good policy ignore them all inconsistent naming rowsums vs as data frame you ll need to learn the quirks python does have first class support over r for certain fields in machine learning r has a terrible type system this probably doesn t mean anything to you right now but it ll be a pain of you re writing production code i recommend that you learn r you ll need to at some point if you re interested in data but also add python to your to learn list']
