Name: 237_facebook_zuckerberg_products_google
Representative_Docs: ['what criteria are you using to base your comparison on though cause this seems like a very apples to oranges kind of comparison another thing is all those companies you list are very much in the public eye tons of medical biotech companies are not so unless you actively follow the industry or read medical journals you probably aren t even going to be aware of what kind of innovation is going on in the space also you mentioned in another comment that some findings in physics don t contribute anything to people which i mean i disagree with for one it s an important confirmation so future theories and applications can be built around it but i m more interested in how you re evaluating things on how they meaningfully impact peoples lives what innovative and meaningful impact has facebook had that they ve turned people into products and invade people s privacy even if they re not a user of the service or would it be the negative impact it s had on people s mental well being to the point that on average every time someone uses facebook they re less happy than before they looked at it if it just comes down to scale in what way revenue well facebook had b last fy while johnson amp johnson largest pharmaceutical company had b or is it employees facebook has k employees while johnson amp johnson has k also if it s scale i m surprised spacex and tesla even made it on your list considering they re quite small in comparison even in their respective industries but sure you ll probably point out why am i using facebook as the example why don t i use alphabet or amazon so alphabet s revenues and employee count were b and k and amazon is b and k now surely that s the epitome of scale right well not quite there s other public companies that are equivalent or even larger even in tech for instance what about ping an huge insurance company in china but invest a lot into tech and are into other financial services and have the largest ai assisted telehealth platform in china at m users and k daily consultations b and k or surprised you didn t even list apple given they nearly top the list at b and k and then if we go back to taking a look outside of tech none of these come close to the scale of something like walmart with their b and m so i m really not even sure what you re using to infer scale but even with that said scale does not mean innovation some large companies spend more on r amp d than others but quite often much of the innovation comes from outside through acquiring smaller companies also even if we take a look at what kind of things in tech might be considered innovative having or will have a large impact often times this is leveraging work done by many different groups for instance a lot of the work in ai is based on work that comes from statisticians mathematicians neuroscientists etc honestly it s kind of naive and arrogant to assume your industry is the only one that s making an impact or is innovative the lines between many industries is constantly blurring so you ll find tech being utilised everywhere and the work done is often contributed by many different people of different backgrounds not just people with a cs background', 'gt big company problems i see but i do have problems wrapping my head around a few things instead of fixing a few things like having your comment notifications go directly to your comment they keep adding and adding features another person says it makes them money but wouldn t a developer want to keep persons using their app an not feeling frustrated it might sound like i m whining but i m genuinely trying to analyze the process many probably do i bet you a few don t here i can t comment on facebook in particular s process but i know other companies have a very particular way of deciding how to focus that involves the devs at varying degrees at differing points in the process gt for example google has to manage oss besides youtube hangouts ai angular etc etc a google program rarely ever feels unintuitive unless they make some weird ui change like they do to youtube really frequently i rarely see google bugs i dont think facebook has the number of products that google has yet their software doesn t really feel that finished aside from the ones they aquire a few things here i definitely have a lot of issues with different google products my android phone foremost for example i used to use voice control exclusively for adding things to my todo lists one day that broke forever i also used to activate my phone by saying computer until they fixed that i think the way you use google products is working well for you by chance the more important issue though that i m trying to get across is what it means to say google has to manage two oss youtube hangouts etc what that means in a company with a hierarchical structure is that there are multiple trees of people in the company dedicated to different sub problems at the heads of that tree are execs who have very high level views of their products they decide what the goals are and they determine what problems they ll try to solve that year they have reports who are tasked with making those goals come to fruition as well as helping decide what the goals are now imagine you re at a point in the year after you ve put work into deciding what youtube is going to focus on let s say youtube s goal for is to make the youtube creator studio dark mode ready put yourself in a position where you ve planned out how to do that work and it just lines up with the the end of the year in that position it makes no sense to sacrifice the goal you have to fix something that just nags people but its been around forever maybe next year you can decide that you ll dedicated of your time to fixing legacy issues like that but you don t want to pivot away from something high value pair that with people coming and leaving big companies over time and having a high cost of development because of requiring various integrations into other company services gt why among software giants does facebook have one of the worst feeling products when that product is their flagship i think i disagree with the premise again is amazon s retail website really the best website they do make things easy for customers but if you go to a detail page for a product you ll end up seeing things pop in and out of existence as you scroll you ll see different styles in different parts of the return process weird ancient dialogs if you wander off into a low traffic area i think facebook s design feels stale but i don t think other big companies are in a whole different league compared to it you should definitely check out ads google com i think that might change your mind about google being great and make you narrow it down to google search is great', 'alright i ll step back a bit then i recognize that there was more vitriol than warranted certainly but i expect people in the industry to be more cognizant of the sheer magnitude of the difficulties involved here you say facebook doesn t respect user privacy and yet for users it s possible to download all the information that facebook collects about them this is a massive undertaking just imagine how many data sources had to be aggregated how many teams this involved and how many resources this service consumes it s literally the equivalent of launching an entire product sure in terms of data collection there are huge swaths of data collected that people are entirely not cognizant of the news for example that the app has the ability to log your calls and texts and i m definitely not condoning this i think it s really bad but how do you craft a policy about this let s say facebook logs when people open their news feed simply for the purpose of debugging production outages you re going to need a couple things going user ids timestamps ip address user agent strings to identify browser and device network edge latency how far back do you keep this data days days a quarter a year you need aggregate data for capacity planning you can t just aggregate into qps though you also need to track regional qps to identify regions where user adoption is accelerating most rapidly you also need to track browser device type to better inform broader technical decisions and direction the modern pivot to mobile means developers need to build performant web apps with support for async loading because hopefully high throughput is possible but latency will be shit for years to come you need to track click through rates to other websites to demonstrate that facebook is an important traffic referral point for them and so the list goes on where does the consumer fit into this are you going to give them a page full of a million yes no checkboxes where users indicate their consent as to what information you may record you obviously can t do this that s a massive cognitive burden for the user besides even if you did would they understand the implications of each yes no answer how do you ask about the duration of information retention how many users even think about this are you obligated to inform users when their data gets stored in a backup and youtube with all its conspiracy and gore videos as a quick reminder of scale youtube has over b monthly active users see the alphabet q earnings call serves billions of views daily over the past four years creators and other partners have earned gt b through the ecosystem so first off how do you define a conspiracy or gore video sure a flat earther manifesto is probably a conspiracy theory type video and videos about the pale blue dot aren t but what about a documentary about the sociology of conspiracy theories what if it s been broken up into n parts where part of happens to consist of minutes expert discussion minutes of clips of different people spouting different conspiracy theories what if it s a series where sociologists interview conspiracy theorists and the sociologists go along with it for the sake of developing behavior profiles and each video is one interview or on another side what about someone speculating about internal politics at say news corp or fox news and some people are eerily on point while others are wildly off base what do you do with someone like alex jones who has millions of viewers and says alexa you are lying to me on screen and as for gore videos what about clips of that scene from alien or the monster from pan s labyrinth are those gore what about a video of someone in their bedroom describing a fantasy world styled after dante s inferno where they spend minutes describing in excruciating detail a specific type of very exotic punishment involving medieval torture tools even if you do have a magic context aware oracle that classifies videos for you what do you do with that information do you outright ban them surely censorship isn t the answer because humans are intelligent creatures right or if it is what happens when snowden v decides to blow the whistle via livestream and let s remember that you can also throw the monetization lever before you throw the censorship one how do you handle that what kind of precedent does say demonetizing someone like logan paul set and this is before you mix in all the thorny issues that come with touching politics all this sounds extreme but let s remember the Name: 244_plagiarism_copyleaks_detectors_copy leaks
Representative_Docs: ['so there are always two starting points ai detectors vibes these are staring points not evidence in and of themselves if either of these two things are telling you that a piece of writing is ai that s when you investigate further ideally further investigation should be handled by a centralized academic ethics office because they can usually check their work across classes at our campus it is standard protocol to turn suspected ai use to these offices and they re generally good about doing their due diligence if you don t have a centralized office here are some steps you can take check every writing sample from the student you have often times this is all it takes i often see students whose essays are very polished but flag for ai meanwhile they re turning in very rough discussion board posts that flag as human written that s a good indicator they re using ai on certain types of assignments because it gives you a baseline to compare to in a similar vein check assignments that require personal or anecdotal information that ai can t reproduce well it often will either have a distinctly different writing style from their other work or it will be way too generic and obviously not human written check the metadata of submissions if the total editing time is shorter than it should be for the length of the document it means it was probably pasted from another source in part or in whole that in conjunction with a high ai score is fairly good evidence at the final step have a conversation with the student start by them specific questions about the content what did you mean when you said x which one of your sources most closely aligns with your opinion on your topic stuff like that when you talk with the student include a question about something that wasn t included in their paper writing assignment and see if they still try to bs an answer e g on this page you wrote about y why did you include that in your argument by this point students often know they re caught and have already confessed to me if they haven t tell them that you suspect them and why if they still don t confess and you still suspect them turn it into administration and let them make the final judgment i hope that helps ai in my opinion is a helpful tool but shouldn t be tolerated in academic writing because it undercuts the learning process and becomes a crutch for researchers of all levels i hope you find ways to effectively encourage formative writing in your classes', 'if i suspect then i check with multiple detectors they are unreliable yes but a plagiarism detectors are unreliable too but i use those reports as a starting place not a final judgement and b if there is smoke from multiple detectors there is usually at least a small fire somewhere but not always so i then keep checking at this point i review the document history looking for edits usually i ll see big paste jobs with few edits not definitive proof but interesting next i plug my prompt into multiple generative ai programs if answers are close same organization same points arguments similar language then i screen shot those similarities at this point i now have pieces of evidence plus my own professional opinion i then reread the paper and note any language or vocabulary that seems like the student might not know for example i had a student in a research proposal say they were doing a regression analysis not impossible but noteworthy then i look closely at their sources i then schedule a meeting with the student and in the meeting i calmly and in a non accusatory manner ask them questions about the paper including asking them about the technical terms they use e g how will a regression analysis work in the project you propose if they can t answer the questions at all or even tell me what the term means i then present to them the evidence i have collected pointing toward generative ai use the combination of it all is pretty damning and the student usually admits to it at that point if not i send a report with my evidence to the office that deals with student conduct they do their own testing with different detectors and so on if detectors flag it generative ai recreates their paper or at least parts of it there is no edit history in their doc another office reaches the same conclusion as me and the student doesn t know the terms or concepts they are writing about all that in combination is pretty good evidence of ai use it s of course a lot of work to do all this but i don t mind i do not want to accuse anyone of academic dishonesty without a lot of evidence and i teach classes where writing is essential this is just now part of my job description', 'are you frustrated with obvious ai student posts or papers that don t register high enough in an ai fake detector to give a for ai plagiarism i e certainty if so i found a clever way to catch those students i noticed that when a post paper gets scanned for ai and registers something like it is usually because the student wrote the first sentences by hand to describe the prompt but then the rest is ai written i ve found if you omit the sentences that describe your prompt or are obviously human created a scan of the rest of the paper will often show ai likelihood which does allow us to give a on that paper if your syllabus warns them that ai papers get a this trick works so well that in my two async online classes this semester the number of posts i found and flunked for being ai written went up by about and i found that by rooting out all the ai papers early on many students start to write their own papers and the others eventually get dropped for continually turning in ai work if your syllabus defines attendance as completing all the week s work and you note that ai work does not count towards attendance also because the free ai detection tools of gptzero me and copyleaks com only allow a certain amount of posts each month i got a paid copy of copyleaks com for a month that allows for scans a month and has been invaluable in catching ai posts you can pause your sub during the summers and i paste a copy of the scan results right below the student posts to let the rest of the class know i m as ai saavy as they are sniping tool in windows makes it easy another trick i use to be sure the person is using ai is to get a real sample of their actual writing in the first casual post it s basically what is your major and why are you taking this course then when you get a student who struggles with english grammar in their intro but is writing like a phd in later posts you have an extra layer of security that what you are getting is ai written another trick i have to catch ai cheats is whenever i assign an article that is a classic thah is all over the web i copy it into a word document and renumber the pages from or whatever then in my prompt i instruct them to use the repaginated article i stored in the module then when you see they support their claims with direct quotes from the article with exact pages given you can spot in a few seconds ai written papers because they are using the pages from version online not the one you altered i don t accuse them of ai i just paste in message announcing they got a o since none of their quotes could be found on the pages they claimed thus weren t valid one note of warning my methods probably won t work on the more savvy students who run their ai posts through humanizer paraphrasing websites till they sound human nor will the repagination trick i mentioned above work if the students upload the article into unstuck ai but you will catch the really lazy ones who just use chatgpt or grammerly or whatever also i did a lot of research and in empirical testing copyleaks and turnitin com had the best results at identifying machine learning and the lowest rate of false positives and i learned the way it works is it looks for key phrases that machine learning and generative ai use and give stats on how much more likely a machine is to use such a term than a human it will flag lofty phd sounding phrases that students are unlikely to use as something from x more likely to be machine written than human finally since unstuck ai can t read text or data from a jpeg image to prevent them from using ai to answer questions in my prompts i will often refer them to a slide in the slide packet that is a jpeg image rather than plain text and then ask them to use theory to explain the numbers or some other such request this isn t something ai can do yet good luck on your quest to keep our students intellectually honest and thinking']

Name: 245_education_going away_future_enhancing education
Representative_Docs: ['i like to anticipate the next cultural shift but all paths i see for us in the future lead to us not having to learn much if anything a personal wearable ai can guide you through learning just what you need to learn memorization is already becoming unnecessary scarily ai is getting better every day and it was already good a year ago even if you don t use ai fully you re still using it to correct yourself and edit your work what percentage of use is too much it doesn t have to be perfect just functional to replace people the tech is competing with the student which is your sign that its already won schools are more regulated than companies so if it s bad in class it s way more rampant in the real world corporatism demands efficiency so it s not going away if ai is good enough to fool the teacher then maybe it should be the teacher this new era of humanity is going to be a big shift and if we re not careful it could be our downfall we re not being careful', 'ai is not going away is not a basis for an educational model because educational models need to be centered on cognitive and behavioral development not the release of something as transformative as the slap chop i mean ai we absolutely need to bring children into a world wherein ai exists and has increasingly complicated roles in society but that s very different from working from a position that centers edtech products as solutions by default or presupposes that the inevitable interactions of a workforce and adult population with ai means that we throw these tools at children or reinvent educational models it is possible that the most necessary response is actually a contraction away from these tools to ensure the validity of assessment of the fundamentals skill acquisition content knowledge narrowing of the affective and epistemic divides required for children to make meaning of things', 'i think we re facing a problem of misaligned incentives education especially in k to is fundamentally relational it is built on connection trust and care but much of the current push around ai in education is being driven by a speculative venture backed mindset this mindset assumes we can scale learning a hundredfold automate teaching and revolutionize schools with chatbots and video libraries that vision appeals to people chasing returns in the tech sector not to those grounded in the daily realities of students and educators students are not just empty vessels waiting to be filled with content they need a reason to care they need to feel seen supported and part of a learning community in many classrooms today teachers are overwhelmed and students are worn down by systems that prioritize compliance over curiosity if used well ai could help restore time and space for the human parts of education relationships reflection and deep engagement but that would require a different set of priorities right now we are working within a large system built on outdated assumptions those assumptions are already beginning to break the way forward is not about greater efficiency for its own sake it is about rethinking the purpose of education changing education at a structural level will take time and it will not happen through shortcuts or quick fixes but if we realign our incentives around care connection and meaningful learning ai could help create something truly transformative']

Name: 250_data structures_structures_classes_algorithms
Representative_Docs: ['for reference out of your list this was at my mid tier school in iowa the classes i got to take programming fundamentals for engineers basics of programming in c computer science fundamentals basics of compile time runtime scope the stack and head data structures amp algorithms self explanatory theory of computation turing and other s work on dfa nfa turing machines p vs np etc digital logic systems fpga programming software design design patterns software construction version control environments pipelines etc microprocessors amp microcomputers learning assembly basically networking principles amp protocols osi model data link network transport and app layers and their protocols tons of cisco labs operating systems scheduling memory pages etc web technologies mean stack building full stack web apps human computer interface design ux design and vr software requirements and analysis basically how to work with pms computer network applications basically just socket programming software engineering design i basic system architecture design software project amp process management waterfall scrum agile etc organization of programming languages writing scanners and parsers from scratch artificial intelligence i basic pac man ai and common regression models computer graphics i rasterization and ray tracing from scratch in c artificial intelligence ii neural nets deep learning software engineering design ii complex architecture design software testing amp maintenance unit integration end to end regression cloud computing learned aws azure and google cloud for tradition web apps serverless data pipelines etc information security encryption hashing common attacks etc so essentially it can be a bit of a crapshoot my school wasted a few classes not really properly teaching us anything i had a class that was supposed to focus around ui ux but went completely off the rails for some reason there was a class around design patterns but the class was horrible there was a computer architecture class i took that was so easy that we learned almost nothing we had a senior project that basically completely failed and yet there were no repercussions but i still think i learned a lot just not as much as maybe i would ve hoped for', 'so the most important are the first semesters of going from zero to learning how to write programs i m assuming you re not asking about those as they re among the ones that are required at any university for a bachelor s or minor in cs for these which path you take probably isn t that important unless some language you learn helps you in a later class what is important here is to learn how to write small programs bonus if you can also learn how to read parts of or use larger programs also any data structures or algorithms you encounter in this part are the important algorithms after that anything on the systems side these were the classes i enjoyed the least as an undergrad and they re also the ones that turned out to be useful in industry or for some systems classes ended up wishing i had taken when the relevant topics came up in industry systems here include os computer architecture networks databases compilers distributed systems storage systems etc after that application courses in application domains i ended up working in were useful like taking a graphics class is pretty important if you want to work in graphics yes you could teach yourself the course is probably easier than teaching yourself though then after that programming languages the important part here is the experience of learning a little bit of a bunch of languages quickly and some theoretical bits that allow you to learn broad classes of languages more easily after that because i worked in graphics linear algebra after that a whole bunch of math that only ended up mattering because i intentionally sought out application domains that needed them of these statistics is the one i d recommend to the widest swath of would be programmers if you re going into ml or ai more statistics is always better if you re going into robotics any math that s useful to mechanical engineering or controls is now useful to you most programmers don t work in these areas then any algorithms or discrete math i took beyond the second semester data structures and discrete math courses where this will benefit you the most other than in interviews is when you have to read a description of an algorithm implement it and understand what it s strengths and weaknesses are if you re lucky every once in a while you ll have an excuse to invent a new algorithm but you re usually under enough time pressure that you want to avoid that whenever it s faster to look up an existing algorithm or better yet an existing implementation of an existing algorithm the other way in which these courses are useful is that by building on earlier data structures and algorithms courses they end up ensuring that you know the basics from your first data structures course or two very very well oh and there s some overlap between specialized areas of algorithms and distributed systems or concurrency both of which are nearly unavoidable in industry', 'gt while cs has the data structures and algorithms courses know i ve heard it a thousand times well here s the nd time you ll hear about it as another post mentioned data structures and algorithms is easily the most important course for back end development because you want to know how to process a pile of data from customers or from a thousand web site visitors efficiently this course is also the most common fodder for technical job interview questions the other useful ones from the cs is computer architecture which is a must for embedded systems development honestly the cs course looks like the one that s better for software development that software development course has a whole bunch of stuff thrown into a big bag want to be an system admistrator it applications okay so a dedicated course in how people use information technology systems looks like a great preparation to tell you all the stuff you ll be an administrator for introduction to communication to deal with all the support tickets and people yelling at you that their e mail doesn t work data management foundations data management applications so database administrator network and security foundations gotta configure that firewall eh want to be a product manager business of it project management business of it applications out of nowhere a project management course appears organizational behavior and leadership random jumble of programming related courses applied probability and statistics this is an oddball inclusion probability and statistics would be great for a machine learning task whenever i have a machine learning task the team spends more time with statistics than algorithm code because statistics is how you churn a number that says the algorithm is good however what s all that math going to do if you re missing courses needed to apply that math to a nice algorithm you need any one of the following data structures and algorithms nosql fundamentals of distributed systems fundamentals of artificial intelligence basics of data structures software ii advanced java concepts okay maybe this is data structures and algorithms in disguise honestly it s used for an hr filter so often on students that they should have just named it data structures and algorithms with java the other possibility since i m just guessing is that the software engineering course could be talking about java ee or spring boot apps in which case again you need that data structures and algorithms gem to get an understanding of why pointing spring data at your terabyte database is slow']

Name: 252_pittsburgh_nyc_chicago_city
Representative_Docs: ['i live in colorado now but used to live in ohio an hour away from pittsburgh i still visit often pittsburgh is solid but not as well established as other tech hubs yet imo you can get triple figure salaries depending on the company you work for and housing cost of living is comparatively low i live in denver its in a nice sweet spot where you can command k as a mid senior engineer but good bedrooms are still findable at a month good houses are findable for k lots of interesting tech between downtown denver and boulder and a good amounts of established companies in the valley have satellite offices here slack amazon google twitter apple gusto twilio etc columbus is up and coming as a midwest tech city i can say from experience that it still has a ways to go though and it doesn t have as sound fundamentals or anchors as something like pittsburgh cmu and computer vision expertise or denver proximity to boulder and broad amounts of tech talent lots of high hobbies that make it appealing for talent', 'nyc can get expensive mostly due to taxes and rent you pay state nyc tax if you punch your numbers assuming you r single your effective state local tax is gonna be near and rent is high you ll probably pay k more per month in manhattan versus downtown seattle car is not necessary tho in fact it ll be very expensive to do so cuz parking so you save some money on car maintenance insurance paying to get around via subway or bus is cheap living in nyc is lit and quite an experience and you are paying more for great quality of life if you like the hustle and bustle of nyc many bloomberg new hire get a roommate and live in manhattan which is still expensive but awesome fun times if you do go bloomberg don t cheap out and live in one of the burrows you ll hate yourself for having to crowd in the trains just to get to work and then going home to your dank ass burrow get a roommate or a studio near the office within walking distance enjoy life if money is really a stretch then move out to another are or dif company later but you won t regret living it up in manhattan for a few years you ll def save a buck in seattle but is it really worth it if you prefer nyc i d say the money is close enough to not matter in the long run so you know you prefer nyc already just gota figure out if the culture work experience is worth it i know people at bloomberg than nyc and they liked the culture good wlb there s tons of teams so if you wanna do infrastructure python node js machine learning you can probably land into the team right off the bat after training most people get placed in one of their top choice but you can easily request to move teams in a year easily too if you missed it first time around some people whine that bloomberg is all old tech and c but idk from what i can tell there are a lot more options than that opportunities after bloomberg seem good too i know of two that left and one google and the other to citadel also people seem pretty happy there for most part average tenure is seem long and when they do leave it seem to be into really great companies donno much about salesforce but they seem good culture wise too and the tech seem to be good but again others will give you better info about salesforce than me btw did you do bloomberg discovery day or have one coming up', 'i ve been in chicago a couple years out of graduation dunno if i ll be here long term but it s been nice so far just because all my friends from school are here and it s the most affordable big city you can get quite the bang for your buck here tech here is definitely more limited than the typical coastal cities sf seattle nyc boston but there s definitely a couple niches here you can find yourself in they are finance trading chicago is the best place for this outside of nyc tbh and it s definitely the area where you can make the most it s not for everyone tc can be highly dependent on how the firm does culture is more intense than laid back sf some longer hours but does offer best earning potential and good long term career growth the best way in is a good math algo background and grabbing an internship your junior year traditional tech companies these are mostly satellite offices of west coast firms with a couple of chicago grown thrown in that offer what silicon valley has to offer on a smaller scale pay is good especially when taking into account col but not quite finance examples include google braintree flexport stripe groupon venmo capital one career growth is pretty good within the company and you can easily move to other offices if you want but limited selection here compared to other tech hubs can definitely limit hopping the consulting firms amp investment banks chicago is a huge management consulting amp banking city and all of these firms are ramping up their digital sectors ai etc pay can be quite good if you rise up the ranks the hours travel definitely aren t for everyone though i think it is definitely easier to get a job here out of school than some of the above fwiw examples include mckinsey bcg jp morgan citi accenture etc startups there are quite a few small companies with really solid dev culture here but i will say they are harder to find they hire a lot based on network and until you build up your network it s hard to say find a list of these online but i know quite a few that have really good culture and pay well everything else other companies that are not software focused but need devs the need for engineers here is pretty big and i m constantly spammed with recruiters trying to get me here generally don t pay quite as well as the above but with chicago s col you won t be struggling even here hope this helps']

Name: 255_buzzword_ml_just buzzword_ai buzzword
Representative_Docs: ['depends on the district definition the buzz term ai is a meme the field of science that is working towards incorporating advanced machine learning methods with physical or in some cases digital task completion is legitimate the concept of intelligence is fluid and as we become conditioned to robots and automated task completion our expectations of what is artificially intelligence shift btw pac man examples form level classes are pretty far behind the current bleeding edge look at some of the stuff openai is doing there is also some creepy image processing work that s been done over the last year general purpose ai is however not there yet', 'ai and ml have just become words people to toss around without even understanding what they are just the other day i was in call for an ml project i had initiated in my team as part of a hackathon my leads are happy to make it a full time project in but a progeam manager who wanted to propose it in different words reached out so in my project we created a model to label tickets i e understand what the ticket is and do an action now we can leverage this to an action component which can take this ticket further to augment it or close it but for this we need to code the action component separately pm had this brilliant idea that we can use ml to create the action component like isn t that what machine learning is the machine should be able to write test and deploy the code whenever it sees an new action which it has never encountered before i and a colleague had to explain to him for over mins that this is not ml i mean it s possible to have a program write another program but it would be a lot of effort for barely any result and it is definitely not ml he didn t get the point in the end but accepted that it is not worth the effort so yeah ml is going to take software developer jobs soon', 'most definitely the only reason i use the word ai is it s a nice buzzword and most people can t tell the difference it s a bit like vr what we currently have is at best an immersive screen in your face the problem is about popular language and it s honestly a losing battle nobody wants to hear yeah we got this really neat thing we can do now it s the same as years ago but like our computers can actually run that stuff now which is the reality of ml not to say that there wasn t any progress at this point my approach to the word ai is that it s a buzzword yes but i see it more as a general category that aside while i have my own ideas of what ai is i think people often misunderstand what ml is because we call it learning or training the machine doesn t learn anything it just creates a bigger reference database it doesn t actually create a database it s more of number crunching layers but it s a good enough approximation of how close it is to intelligence there s no reasoning there s no learning and that s why things aren t explainable either that s all my personal opinion and experience though']

Name: 259_students_calendar_ask ai_teach
Representative_Docs: ['so if this isn t enough to scare you off using ai i don t know what is it cannot do anything correctly so why would it be able to double check your work correctly case in point this morning i wanted to start working on my spring semester course and i decided to just test out ai to make me literally just the list of course dates based on our academic calendar my prompt was this is a tuesday thursday course that aligns with university x s spring calendar please give me a list of meeting dates that i can plug into my syllabus and note dates we don t have class for breaks it was literally wrong despite knowing the university and our schedule being online it got spring break wrong it did a bunch of weird stuff and it was so freaking confident i fixed it obviously but this should be the sort of mindless busywork it excels at right wrong trust ai it is making our brains mushy and not even saving us any time in the end i could have made this calendar from scratch and not had to go through this stupid extra step', 'i m also a teacher in grad school and much to my surprise and dismay my professors are implicitly or explicitly encouraging the use of ai i m taking three classes this summer and none of the syllabi explicitly address ai usage which at this point i interpret to mean they find it acceptable the school district i work at is also busy pushing ai solutions on us and blocks netflix it s too distracting but not chatgpt it s the future in my classroom i ve been using lockdown browser for on demand essays but it appears a few students have managed to get around that too i ve done handwritten essays before which reveals the utter lack of fine motor skills and dexterity among students some students literally can t write or type they have to use Name: 262_edtech_education_ecosystem_project based
Representative_Docs: ['i m a passionate rd grade chicago public school teacher who has become increasingly concerned about our education system i strongly believe we re failing our students and desperately need to modernize our approach i m particularly excited about the potential of ai vr and other cutting edge ed tech to revolutionize the way we teach and learn i want to be at the forefront of this transformation but i m not sure where to start i m considering a master s degree in educational technology but i m wondering if there might be better routes to get involved in this field some questions i have is a master s degree worth it or are there more practical alternatives are there specific areas of ed tech that are particularly promising or in demand how can i gain hands on experience with emerging technologies in education are there any organizations or communities you d recommend joining any advice or personal experiences you can share would be incredibly helpful i truly am eager to learn and contribute to shaping the future of education thanks in advance for your insights', 'x b curious about the exciting changes happening in education edtech or educational technology is all about using new tools and platforms to improve how we teach and learn it s like a wave of innovation that s transforming education read how edtech has the power to change education for the better making it more inclusive and exciting thanks to technology we now have amazing digital solutions that make learning more engaging and interactive imagine virtual classrooms where you can learn and have fun at the same time it s like playing games while gaining knowledge teachers also benefit from edtech with the help of data analytics and artificial intelligence they can understand how their students are doing and tailor their teaching methods accordingly it s like having a personal coach who helps you learn better edtech doesn t just happen in traditional classrooms it s breaking barriers and reaching learners everywhere online learning platforms bring education to your fingertips no matter where you are in the world now anyone can access quality resources and learn from expert teachers but edtech isn t only for students it s for professionals too in today s fast changing job market it s important to keep learning and adapting edtech offers flexible courses and certifications that help you stay ahead and seize new career opportunities x b love to hear feedback on what you think of it check out', 'the online learning industry is expected to grow over the next few years due to a variety of factors a research report published by research and markets in december estimates that the industry will swell to billion in up from billion in education ecosystem a startup in the online education landscape is planning to revolutionize the sector by targeting intermediate tech professionals and college students online learning involves the offering of courses and learning materials through the internet by virtual institutions with the aim of equipping students with the knowledge that they can or cannot find from brick and mortar institutions online education as it exists today is purely supplemental to primary traditional education and does not replace the latter completely online education was pioneered by universities that provided course material to students who couldn t attend campuses however new startups and institutions were founded to serve this purpose and provide services that universities and colleges cannot provide alone the biggest online companies in the world are based in asia with china and india respectively taking the lead in online education the industry is growing due to practical and effective education internet penetration a growing base of smartphone users and flexibility that the online learning model offers to students and those who want to further their careers unexpected events such as disease outbreaks can potentially shape the future of the industry the ongoing coronavirus epidemic that broke out in wuhan china could play a huge role in promoting the growth of online education some parents have signed online classes for their children so that the students forced to remain at home can keep up with their peers although this is a temporary measure it could potentially make online learning acceptable to parents who may have been against online education education ecosystem and project based learning education ecosystem ledu is a project based learning platform that teaches professional developers and students how to build real products in areas such as programming game development artificial intelligence cybersecurity data science and blockchain the platform s ecosystem is powered by content creators who produce practical projects that teach intermediate level professionals and students how to build real world products the startup s goal is to build the world s biggest project based learning platform breaking down what makes education ecosystem unique education ecosystem does not have direct competitors but has indirect competitors such as lynda and pluralsight the differences between education ecosystem and its competitors include target users education ecosystem is a career focused learning platform whose target audience are intermediates or experts this is unlike its competitors that focus on beginners and intermediates beginners should start with other online learning platforms so that they master the basics they can then switch to education ecosystem when they are ready to put their knowledge into practice through project based learning this makes education ecosystem s platform ideal for professionals or students who have completed beginner studies learning model the majority of online learning platforms use courses as a method of teaching this works for them because their goal is to teach beginners step by step until they have a good grip on what they are learning this is good though it is a generic approach education ecosystem uses a project based model that allows intermediate level professionals to work on a project it gives intermediates a feel of what it s like to build industry grade products and also help professionals to hone their skills so that they can find success in their careers this sentiment is shared by education ecosystem s project creation manager dusan kolic who said project based learning seeks to better prepare students for solving real life problems and issues while teaching them what they need to know to succeed in their careers learning type education ecosystem uses a practical production level work approach instead of theoretical learning preferred by other platforms this is partly due to the audience that these platforms are targeting other learning platforms lay the foundation while education ecosystem translates the theoretical knowledge into practice decentralized peer to peer model education ecosystem employs a decentralized technology as the bedrock of its platform education ecosystem has built an ecosystem that incentivizes learners project creators api developers other online companies colleges and nonprofits complete projects products the main idea behind education ecosystem s teaching is the end result it focuses on complete projects that offer value to intermediates and professionals rather than teaching theory content creation cost creating content on education ecosystem is fast and cost effective because it is based on user generated peer to peer projects this allows a wider pool of project creators to create projects that benefit learners content creation speed due to the project based nature of the learning material less time is required to create quality practical learning resources for the target audience ryan schuetz was attracted to the platform due to the speed of creating content all i had to do was create a tutorial on something that i have experience in i completed the project python data analytics and visualization in only four days benefits of project based learning project based learning is a great teaching method that provides benefits to both learners and project creators learners focus on one project at a time giving them enough time to analyze the problem and how best they can approach it students gain invaluable experience on how to handle a project from start to finish this includes analyzing the problem from a practical point of view to developing a solution that works for that problem project based learning prepares students for their professional careers by learning from instructors who have vast practical knowledge in specific areas uk based project creator and software engineer elliott minns says that students are more engaged as the idea of building a real product acts as an incentive students see the completion of a real product as an incentive for learning this motivates them and gives them enough reason to sharpen their skills said timms']

providing advanced value and taking smart leaps ahead not talking about nerd shit i have the policy that ai needs to be given a chance to write all non critical documents people should not be writing they should be encoding value through ai and then decoding it using ai summary doing the minimal amount possible so you can focus on face to face communication with customers you re going to be expected to do more because ai will be helping you one person can now do the job of yes it is the future much like most previous industrial revolutions', 'some exec somewhere in the company decided everyone needs to be talking to ai and they track how often you re talking with it to me this just feels like idiotic executives if it weren t ai it would be back to office or lines of code produced etc ai is just another way for a dumb exec to be dumb what happened to actually knowing things when will people realize ai is frequently confidently wrong i use ai and i think i know things i think knowing things is critical in fact i d say my job is now much more about knowing things and a lot less about typing things i need to know the domain the shape of the problem the ux the algorithms that solve it where things can go wrong etc the ai is basically just really fast at making changes across n files which is something that i find harder to do i also personally use ai primarily for writing tests based on the spec i ve given i typically write a few myself and then ask it to continue that forward which i then review i don t think ai is that frequently wrong but to some degree it s a matter of prompting context and experience it definitely is right of the time i ve even had it point out when i m wrong which is super helpful oftentimes the ai can have context that i don t because we create global rules across teams as we learn new lessons over time i feel like an insane person shouting on every company survey and in every town hall meeting to get these ai pilled people to understand the damage they are doing well if you re actually shouting at people at every company survey then yes that is quite odd what have you actually tried you re saying people are adding a lot of defects that s surprising i wonder if maybe you would be better served by helping people to see this using concrete examples acknowledging that people want to use these tools finding safer ways for them to do this helping them know where ai can be helpful and where it s harmful perhaps creating more rules and context for the ai would be helpful in short i would suggest starting with the assumption that there is value in ai because your teammates seem to feel that there is value rather than asserting that they are wrong try taking on their position and then finding out how to maximize the benefits happy to discuss further i understand that you re in a frustrating position it s certainly crazy to hear that your execs are measuring and reprimanding based on ai usage but maybe i can lend some perspective from someone who s more in the middle']

Representative_Docs: ['gt you re correct in that i do not have a lot of research experience gt gt gt gt i have publications in international conferences i m confused most undergrads have zero can make you some quick money if you would like to start earning i would greatly appreciate you signing up via my referral link', 'paid work sometimes referred to as employment or compensated labor is a cornerstone of modern economies and a central part of most people s lives it refers to any form of work performed in exchange for monetary compensation whether through wages salaries commissions or freelance payments beyond just a means to earn a living paid work shapes social identities economic structures and personal development what is paid work paid work includes full time and part time jobs freelance and gig work contract roles and self employment it can take place in virtually every sector from manufacturing and retail to healthcare education and digital services the defining feature is that the work performed results in direct financial compensation examples of paid work include an employee working hours a week at a company a freelancer designing websites for clients a part time barista at a caf a construction worker paid per project or hourly a rideshare driver earning money per trip importance of paid work economic stability paid work is the primary source of income for individuals and families it enables people to meet basic needs such as food housing education and healthcare and to save or invest for the future social contribution through paid work individuals contribute to the economy and society jobs often support essential services infrastructure innovation and commerce personal growth and identity many people find purpose structure and a sense of identity through their work it can be a source of pride achievement and self worth skills development paid work helps individuals acquire and refine skills gain experience and build professional networks which are important for career advancement types of paid work formal employment typically involves a legal contract steady income and access to benefits like health insurance retirement plans and paid leave examples include government jobs corporate roles and nonprofit employment informal employment often lacks legal protection stability or benefits this can include day laborers some domestic workers or street vendors in many parts of the world self employment and freelancing people who work for themselves rather than an employer this includes small business owners consultants artists and online freelancers gig economy work short term flexible jobs often arranged through digital platforms like uber doordash or fiverr gig work offers freedom but often lacks traditional job protections challenges and considerations wage inequality disparities in income exist across industries regions and demographics raising concerns about fairness and access to opportunities job insecurity especially in gig work and informal employment workers may lack job stability predictable income and benefits work life balance long hours high demands or lack of autonomy can negatively impact workers mental and physical health changing landscape automation remote work and ai are reshaping the nature of paid work this creates both new opportunities and the need for continuous learning and adaptation conclusion paid work is more than just earning money it s a vital component of economic participation personal fulfillment and societal development as the world of work continues to evolve ensuring fair wages equitable access and decent working conditions remains a crucial challenge for policymakers businesses and workers alike by understanding the changing dynamics of paid work individuals can better navigate their careers and contribute meaningfully to the broader economy', 'understanding paid work definition significance and emerging trends in modern economies paid work is the backbone of financial independence and economic development whether formal or informal in offices or online paid work enables individuals to earn an income support their families and contribute to society but as technology advances and labor markets evolve the concept of paid work is undergoing significant transformation what is paid work paid work refers to any activity or service performed in exchange for monetary compensation this includes full time jobs part time roles freelancing consulting and gig based assignments unlike volunteer or domestic work which may be essential but unpaid paid work involves a contractual or transactional exchange work performed for a wage salary or fee key characteristics of paid work remuneration the worker receives payment in cash or kind contractual agreement may be formal employment contract or informal verbal agreement or freelance gig labor exchange involves time skill and effort provided by the worker types of paid work full time employment a standard work arrangement often with benefits like health insurance and paid leave part time employment offers more flexibility typically with fewer benefits and reduced hours freelancing and contract work independent professionals hired for specific projects or timeframes gig work short term task based jobs found through platforms like uber fiverr or doordash remote and online work includes jobs that can be performed from any location via digital tools self employment entrepreneurs or small business owners earning income from their ventures the importance of paid work paid work serves several critical functions economic stability it is the primary source of income for most people social inclusion paid employment often defines social identity and status skill development work environments promote learning and professional growth independence it empowers individuals to make personal and financial decisions in addition societies with higher levels of employment generally enjoy better standards of living stronger economies and more equitable access to resources the changing nature of paid work technology and automation advancements in ai and automation are reshaping the job market routine tasks are increasingly automated while demand grows for digital literacy creativity and problem solving skills remote work the covid pandemic accelerated the shift to remote work many companies now offer hybrid models and entire careers can be pursued from home platform economy platforms like upwork taskrabbit and amazon mechanical turk offer new forms of paid work especially for freelancers and gig workers while flexible these roles often lack job security and benefits universal basic income ubi and job guarantees as traditional jobs evolve or disappear some policymakers are exploring ubi and government backed job programs to ensure income stability challenges in paid work despite its benefits paid work is not without challenges wage inequality gaps between low and high paying jobs continue to widen precarious employment many workers face unstable temporary or low paid jobs lack of benefits gig and freelance workers often lack health insurance retirement plans or paid leave work life balance the rise of connectivity blurs boundaries between work and personal life conclusion paid work is more than just a way to earn a living it is central to human dignity economic participation and societal well being as the nature of work continues to evolve it s crucial that governments businesses and workers themselves adapt to ensure that paid work remains fair inclusive and sustainable in the future']

life i now have my literal dream job working at a company fixing the education system happy to answer any q s you have', 'have been talking to a lot of people in particular those with less than years of corporate work experience who are being impacted by ai and offshoring and finding it very hard to find roles there are a few attributes of work life that will not change no matter how technology gets adopted or shifts workforces in the future one of these attributes is working with people to some extent this includes the aspect of organizational politics but ill post separately on this topic this post is very much about what i have learned about working with people in my last years of my career where i ve worked at all levels in the organization including at the very top a being nice being human being yourself there are going to be colleagues who are super smart good at what they do then the other kids of all these people keep an eye on those who you truly feel good around who have your back you are good human beings ethical kind how do you feel when they are around you when you encounter people who check these boxes off try not to judge them by competency or their organizational levels or their chances of success these are the people who will help you in times of need and will open up the doors of opportunities for you i cannot emphasize more the importance of distinguishing good human beings with smart people they are two separate attributes and a person can have both or just one a good human being always goes permanently in my phone book b collaboration see one of the fundamental principles of good collaboration is win win the most sustainable collaboration is when there is a win on both sides the most sustainable and successful collaboration is when there is both a win on each side and there is an organizational win or value that s getting created when only one person is winning a collaboration is mostly forced so when you are in the situation of having to collaborate start by understanding what the win is for the other person or what it can be similarly be very clear about what the win is for you having this clarified in the beginning will do wonders in your ability to collaborate and for bonus points try to also keep an eye on the organizational value being created what often tends to happen is that we start to work with someone with either no focus on the wins or just with a focus on our own win when wins are directly communicated and discussed the collaboration is much easier c listening listen listen listen as a general rule this is great but there are specific times in your corporate experience when this becomes important in particular when you start a new job we all have a desire to prove to ourselves and to others that we can deliver outsized value in the first few months of your job be the listener it will win you respect of the people around you no one wants to be around a know it all and everyone who has worked for a while likes to know that their experience is valued and listening as a skill is very important in building better relationships d conflict resolution conflicts are important and conflict resolution is a critical interpersonal skill catch conflicts early and be comfortable getting into resolution conversations this was one of the skills i picked up later in my career as i was always a conflict avoidant person i would say i agree but really i didn t it took me a long time to learn to say i disagree and here is why ask yourself how many times you are saying i disagree if its zero you need to work on this skill if its all the time then well maybe you need to listen more e working hard one thing i ve learned is that the people around you will always remember the efforts you made and make a good work ethic goes a long way in building lasting relationships with those around you so when you think of working hard of course the immediate thing we think of is how effective we can be at what we re building but also remember the effect of your peers noticing this and remember this quality about you everyone wants to stay connected to hard working people with strong work ethics i learned later on in my career that my hard work was being noticed by many people who stayed in touch with me and helped me when i needed it i would love to hear about how others feel about this topic i believe that as technology changes the knowledge economy some fundamentals of working with people building alliances and teaming up will become even more important for more advice feel free to see r mobiusengine i am posting there more']

Representative_Docs: ['in relation to this concept of being overhyped one thought i had recently is that many people keep saying that ai is only at the beginning and it s just going to continue developing forever and eventually surpass us while that could be the case one thing that many people seem to be ignoring is that everything has an upper limit humans have an upper limit in their capacity for intelligence as does every other species on the planet there are always extreme outliers but there is a limit there has to be a limit on ai s capacity too since ai is in its infancy i don t think we know what that is yet so far i am honestly not really impressed by it i was playing around with it last night and it made so many mistakes in things i asked it to do in the things i was playing around with almost every single thing i generated had at least one mistake in it i started playing around with it because of that atlantic article that was posted on here a few days ago about students at nyu who used it to skate by classes i suspect that student who used ai for all his assignments had professors that either didn t read his work at all i definitely remember having some professors i suspected didn t even look at what i wrote or were burned out an apathetic it was producing things that were comically wrong for even very basic tasks i was asking it to do', 'there are several things that i m suggesting and will try to keep it short we are rapidly approaching the apex of what we can do with ai using current technology there just isn t the time and space complexity leeway to continue advancement we are yet to discover what we actually need to do to improve ai drastically and that can only be known when the new technology vis quantum computing becomes mainstream something close to true ai whatever that is may actually be beyond human comprehension to develop in that ai will eventually have to develop evolve itself to advance haven t you noticed with current advancements in ai that it is only getting marginally better at solving existing problems that it has seen before rather than solving problems that it hasn t can you tell me why that is i ll be happy to explain if need be the concept of machine learning is far from being new infact it was first raised by alan turing near years ago and i was writing neural nets as an undergrad in the s it just goes to show that the theory that over time ai s advancements will become less and less substantial until we hit that ceiling limited by current technology and human comprehension', 'tbh sometimes experts are too close to see whats happening in their field i know that sounds counterintuitive but it good analogy is the closer you look at something the shorter your horizon gets i know plenty of great software devs who know literally nothing about machine learning and among actual experts in machine learning ai neural nets there is definitely not a consensus one way or the other that said many do think self improving ai is possible so much so there are thousands of people around the world working on ai safety problems personally i think whats likely is not something like a human like mind ai becoming self aware instead i think we will figure out a way to make a narrow ai that is good at making other narrow ais at that point you can get a non conscious non sentient intelligence explosion and if its not controlled properly could destroy us all you dont so much need general intelligence as much as an ai that can make other intelligence which are also narrow in fact arguably this is how our brain works perhaps a general ai is nothing but many narrow neural nets working together and from what i know about neurobiology thats not an unreasonable way to think of our own brains']

integrity review board a resource that allows them to compare an essay to a student s previous work across the entirety of their academic career at our institution this would give us some Name: 275_district_teachers_teacher_pd
Representative_Docs: ['i wonder all the time about the future of ela and ai i m using part of this summer vacation to understand chatgpt and google s ai and programming in general more thoroughly so that i can teach the kids how to use it properly it s a tool that now exists in the world it s going to take a lot of ela teacher s jobs too if our situations were reversed i d get a substitute license asap this summer the initial licensure is what takes the longest and costs the most in most states your background check will be complete your references checked your university transcripts on file adding a provisional endorsement later should be very quick teach a few classes at different schools and in different grades over the summer summer school is usually pretty easy or at least doesn t have the normal stressors of a school year parent conferences department meetings standardized testing lock down drills truancy observations and about a million other things i d take a university course that included time spent in secondary classrooms and general pedagogy before i committed to an alternate route to licensure i d probably try and long term sub in the fall when there are lots of openings because so many teachers wanted to get paid over the summer or just couldn t handle the stress of another school year in the twenty years i ve been teaching my mental and physical health have suffered tremendously the psychiatrist who gives me medication to prevent the recurring school related nightmares says that half of her patients are teachers eighty percent of teachers in my district quit within seven years a much higher dropout rate than our students i used to be a juvenile prison guard i witness more violence daily at an average urban high school to paraphrase dickens it s the best job and the worst job i ve ever had i honestly can t imagine doing anything else at this point maybe i still have some dreams of making a difference and maybe occasionally i do make a difference but i probably keep teaching for the same reasons that professional boxers keep boxing fighting is all i know', 'i see your concern and you make valid points as a new teacher i m sure it is disheartening to see ai take over such a vital role in our profession this is my th year as a teacher years in general education and years in special education i am currently general education ela rd th grades my district introduced us to writable ai for aiding in giving feedback and grading writing grammar assignments i like what i have seen of the software so far that being said i will still have to go into the software and check the feedback and grammar changes ai suggests then i virtually return the work to students for revision students will have their first day monday i may not feel so positive towards this in weeks that is tbd i am guilty of using chatgpt to make test questions on assessments that aligned with my eoy state assessment questioning i have always proofread everything and i often change the wording to reflect academic vocabulary used in rd th grades i feel that if we as teachers don t become familiar with ai that students will get credit for work that isn t theirs it comes down to knowing your students and holding them accountable ai also helps me to prioritize my personal time it would take me hours to create a test that ai can spit out in seconds and then i spend minutes making revisions that s huge for me as an educator again all of your points are valid however if your district is on board with your use of ai use that to your advantage take what you can from it but still be authentic in the way you present the information in your classroom we can only do so much as teachers i spent almost every hour awake outside of school editing papers for many years the only approved ai in my district is the writable software i plan on using the hell out of it i hope you have a great school year please take time for self care throughout the school year i wish someone had told me that when i was years into the profession', 'i teach hs science in the south i can only speak for my district buta few teacher work days in the wave of enthusiasm i m seeing for ai tools is overwhelming we re getting district approved ads for ai tools by email admin and ics are pushing it on us and at least half of the teaching staff seems all in at this point i was just in a meeting with my team and one of the older teachers brought out a powerpoint for our first lesson and almost everyone agreed to use it after a quick scan but it was missing important tested material repetitive and just totally airy and meaningless just slide after slide of the same handful of sentences rephrased with random loosely related stock photos when i asked him if it was ai generated he said of course like it was a strange question then i told the team i might go in a different direction because i wanted to avoid using ai in the classroom and the team lead made a face and told me that all of the lessons she brings for our meetings have been ai generated for the last year i get that we re busy i really do last year i had three preps and was coaching and sponsoring a club but we re a well resourced district and we re payed very well we have banks of tests and powerpoints and handouts not to mention good classroom tech basically all of our grading is fully automated at this point we just don t need to be cutting corners like this the fact of the matter is that most of this ai generated stuff is just not as good it s lazy it doesn t align well with our standards and it s very very obvious to the kids we don t have a leg to stand on to teach them anything about originality academic integrity intellectual honesty or the importance of doing things for themselves when they catch us indulging in it just to save time at work here s a list of the worst ai offences i ve seen in my district since the start of the spring semester last school year an admin sent out an email to staff and parents about a weather event with an obvious chatgpt stub accidently copy pasted to the end an on level english teacher began not even secretly using ai to read student papers and generate comments supposedly she graded based on chatgpt s analysis i spoke to her about this casually and she told me it was just a career necessity admin send staff ai generated emails memos graphics and in one case an entirely ai generated video about our lockdown procedures we were meant to show the students complete with creepy uncanny valley photorealistic people who didn t blink ics openly encouraging us to use ai to write our internal documentation pd goals progress for state bonuses with an optional seminar on prompt engineering mandatory pds about new ai software added to our classlink like tools to convert videos and text into not very good quizzes my acp classes through iteach suggesting on every paper i write that i just have their ai tool make it for me and submit that a teacher sponsoring a club put student artwork through microsoft copilot to clean it up because he thought it looked too unfinished and the kid felt incredibly disrespected and upset another science teacher challenged me on the factuality of one of my lessons chemistry nothing political and said let s check chatgpt if and see what it says our law enforcement teacher told a student they should just use ai to answer the questions on a worksheet since he had made the worksheet with ai anyway this is only my third year in the career so maybe i just don t get it but it feels like this is a cliff that we re just throwing ourselves off of as a profession rant over lol']
